experiments.title: How embedding a donation form impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that embedding the donation form for donation page visitors will achieve more donations because there are fewer steps to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2025 - 08/05/2025
Dallas Theological Seminary recently switched donation platforms. The new default donation page has the “Give Now” button embedded and when the button is clicked the form pops up. We wanted to test embedding the form and skipping the button on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that embedding the donation form directly on the page, rather than requiring donors to click a separate button, resulted in a significant increase in donations. Specifically, the treatment group experienced a 36.9% increase in Transactions for All Traffic with a 99.0% level of confidence. Additionally, One-Time Donations saw a 400% increase, and Revenue increased by 231%, both with high levels of confidence.
These results suggest that reducing the number of steps for donors can increase conversion rates. Future experiments should focus on streamlining the donation process and making it seamless. This could involve optimizing the donation form, testing layouts, or exploring other ways to reduce giving barriers. The goal is to create a frictionless donor experience that boosts donations for the nonprofit.

experiments.title: How using ease language impacts clicks in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using ease language for people who receive the email will achieve more clicks because they will be more encouraged to become a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
When asking donors to consider monthly giving, the way you frame the opportunity can make all the difference. In this test, the team focused on how to talk about ease. Rather than only highlighting the benefits of monthly giving, the copy emphasized how simple and straightforward it is to sign up as a recurring donor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using language about the ease of becoming a monthly donor significantly increased clicks in the email. The treatment, which focused on the ease of becoming a monthly donor, saw a 67.4% increase in clicks for all traffic with a statistical confidence level of 96.5%. This means the hypothesis was supported, showing that people were more encouraged to become a monthly donor when presented with language highlighting the ease of doing so.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the impact of language on donor behavior. Donations like Dallas Theological Seminary can increase donor engagement and acquisition by incorporating ease-of-donation language in future appeals and communications. It is also crucial to analyze the impact of different messaging strategies on other metrics such as donation amount, donor retention, and overall campaign success. Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of using language that resonates with donors and simplifies the donation process to inspire action.

experiments.title: How embedding an email sign up form onto the registration page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the email sign up form to the course registration page will increase the email acquisition because it reduces the friction at the decision point.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2025 - 05/23/2025
Dallas Theological Seminary wants to reach more people worldwide with biblical-truth and teach people how to study the Bible.
We ran this test on the DTS Revelation Free Online Course registration page. The audience: People from social media ads.
We want to know if more people will sign up for the course if the form is embedded on the register page. The original version a person had to click a button to go to the sign up form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the email sign-up form directly to the course registration page significantly increased email acquisition for Dallas Theological Seminary. The treatment group experienced a 307.4% increase in email acquisition compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating that the change had a substantial impact.

In future experimentation, Dallas Theological Seminary should consider simplifying the registration process by reducing the steps required for users to sign up. This can help reduce friction at the decision point and potentially lead to more conversions. Additionally, A/B testing different variations of the registration process, such as the placement of the form or the wording of the call-to-action, can provide valuable insights into what resonates best with the target audience.

Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of optimizing the user experience to make it as seamless as possible for individuals to engage with the organization's offerings, ultimately leading to higher conversion rates and increased participation in their educational programs.

experiments.title: How using more vigorous copy that focuses on providing value to the reader in Facebook ads affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing value through more vigorous copy for Facebook ad recipients will achieve more email acquisition because it will motivate them to take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/23/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary used Facebook ads to promote their free online course How to Read the Bible Like a Seminary Professor. The objective of the ads was to acquire new email addresses. The original ad copy focused on the organization first. Would more vigorous ad copy that provided value to the reader result in an increase in motivation and email acquisition?
experiments.keyLearnings:
Providing value through more vigorous ad copy can significantly increase email acquisition rates. The treatment, which included more engaging and valuable ad copy, resulted in a 148.4% increase in emails acquired compared to the control group. This success was achieved with a high level of statistical confidence at 96.1%.

In future experimentation, it is essential to prioritize providing value to the audience in ad copy. By focusing on how the offering can benefit the reader and addressing their needs and interests, it is more likely to motivate them to take action, such as providing their email address. This approach can be applied to other marketing campaigns to improve results and increase the effectiveness of acquiring new leads or customers.

experiments.title: How adding autonomy-supportive copy to a donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding autonomy-supportive copy for people who have signed up for a course will achieve more donations because donors will feel like they have the freedom to make their decision.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2024 - 08/27/2024
While sending visitors from a free course sign up page to an instant donation page, we noticed the copy on the page could be improved. The copy was rewritten to include things like: a simplified opening, removed redundancy, conversational tone, clarity in donation impact, and consistent gratitude. We hypothesized that this would encourage more donors to make a donations if they felt as if they had more freedom in their decision.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Autonomy-supportive copy significantly increased donations: By providing donors with a sense of freedom in their decision-making process, we were able to increase revenue for all traffic by 69.1% with a high level of statistical confidence. This shows that empowering donors to make their own choices can lead to more positive outcomes.

2. One-Time Donations and Transactions significantly increased: The treatment resulted in a 398% increase in One-Time Donations with a 96% level of confidence, as well as a 385% increase in Transactions with a 95% level of confidence. This indicates that the revised copy not only encouraged more donations but also led to larger contributions in terms of one-time donations.

Moving forward, we should use the learnings from this experiment in future experimentation by continuing to focus on autonomy-supportive messaging in our donation appeals. By emphasizing donors' freedom to choose and the impact of their contributions, we can potentially see continued increases in donations and revenue. Additionally, we should iterate on the successful elements of the revised copy to refine our approach and further optimize our donation pages for maximum impact.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial to enhance authority impacted course signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing appeal, clarity, and authority through a testimonial for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in course registrations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2022 - 11/18/2022
In a previous experiment, we had seen that adding a personal quote from the professor on the course registration page increased sign-ups. We wanted to test this same hypothesis on additional courses to see if this is something that was specific to the previous course or specific quote or if this was a global concept that could be rolled out on all courses taught by the seminary’s president. For this course, we added a brief testimonial and a photo of the professor so potential course registrants can get a glimpse of additional clarity about who is teaching the course and how having social proof from a figure of authority would motivate visitors to give away their email address on the page. We split the traffic 50/50 to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we discovered after running the experiment for one month was that the treatment lifted the conversion rate on the acquisition page by 7.4% with 97.2% confidence. Drilling down into specific segments, we did notice a directional decrease in conversion from paid social, which will need to be addressed. However, on its face we believe this treatment layout makes the course more appealing by humanizing the offer. Since we have now seen similar treatments provide lifts on various courses regardless of the course-specific testimonial content, we will add this feature to the existing layout of the course registration pages for those that are taught by the seminary president.

experiments.title: How a lower gift array impacted conversion on an instant donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: The hypothesis is: can we get more people to say yes to making a donation, if we offer a lower value option in the gift array?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2023 - 12/31/2023
We noticed on a previous content offer donation page many people were entering lower amounts than the $50, $100, and $150 options on the page. While the overall average gift for all conversions is right between $50-$60 which was on track with the previous year’s results, we were seeing a range of manual amounts from visitors of $10, $25, $30 and $45 in the “other” field.
With this in mind, we wondered if it might make sense to develop a treatment adding in a fourth gift array option of $25 for the Christmas devotional instant donation page while still keeping the $50 secondary amount as the pre-selected choice and measure conversion response from visitors.
If we could increase conversion by lowering the entry level amount, this could help us easily course-correct the initial revenue decrease we were seeing so far in the year-over-year comparison and get back on track. We launched the test, splitting traffic 50/50 and ran it for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a lower value option in the gift array can significantly increase the number of people willing to make a donation. The treatment version, which included a $25 option along with the $50 and $100 options, saw a 26.2% increase in transactions for all traffic compared to the control version at a 95.5% level of confidence so the experiment results are valid.
This suggests that some potential donors may be hesitant to give higher amounts and offering a lower option can help overcome this hesitation. By providing more choices and catering to different financial capabilities, nonprofits like Dallas Theological Seminary can encourage more people to contribute.
In future experimentation, it would be valuable to further explore the impact of different gift array options. This could involve testing additional variations, such as offering even lower values or different ranges. Additionally, it would be beneficial to analyze the average gift amounts for each option to understand if the lower value options are affecting overall donation amounts. By continuously iterating and testing different gift array options, nonprofits can optimize their donation pages to maximize conversions and revenue.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity in copy for a sticky bar impacts click through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the messaging and call-to-action in the sticky bar from a generic request for feedback to a more specific and personalized invitation to take a survey about the user’s learning experience will result in a higher click through rate because it is a more tangible and manageable ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2023 - 11/26/2023
Dallas Theological Seminary had a survey that they wanted to advertise on their website to encourage people to provide feedback on DTS free online courses. Previously, they had tested the angle of acknowledging that the person reading the sticky bar was a friend of DTS that became the control.
For this test, we wanted to see if that angle was more impactful or if providing clarity on the length of the survey and extending a specific invitation to the person reading the sticky bar would lead to more clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing a specific and personalized invitation to take a survey resulted in a significantly higher click-through rate compared to a generic request for feedback. The treatment version, which used more specific and tangible language, saw a 343.2% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% level of confidence.

Based on these results, it is recommended to use the learnings from this experiment in future experimentation. When designing sticky bars or any other call-to-action messaging, it would be beneficial to use specific and personalized language that clearly communicates the purpose of the action and the value it provides to the user. This approach can increase engagement and participation rates. Additionally, it is important to continue testing and iterating on messaging to optimize click-through rates and other desired outcomes.

This experiment underscores the impact that small changes in layout can have on user behavior and reinforces the significance of clarity and reducing friction in driving conversions. Building on this learning, future experimentation should continue to focus on optimizing the placement of key elements on landing pages to maximize engagement and drive desired actions from visitors.

experiments.title: How the presentation of the format of a content offer impacts email sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showcasing the digital resource on an iPad mockup on the landing page will lead to an increase in email sign-ups because there is increased clarity on what the person will receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2023
Each year around Christmas, Dallas Theological Seminary creates an eBook devotional to send to their email list.
This year, they wanted to see how the image on the landing page impacts overall email signups. Historically, they have used a 3d eBook image of the cover of the eBook. They wanted to test how showcasing the devotional as a digital resource by mocking it up on an iPad would impact email conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that showcasing the eBook as a digital resource on an iPad mockup on the landing page led to a significant increase in email sign-ups. The treatment group, which saw the iPad mockup, had a 3.9% higher conversion rate compared to the control group, which saw the traditional 3d eBook image.
This indicates that providing a clear visual representation of the digital resource, such as through an iPad mockup, helps potential subscribers better understand what they will receive. This increased clarity likely contributed to the higher conversion rate.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue showcasing the digital resource on an iPad mockup or similar visual representation. This can be applied to other landing pages or promotional materials to improve conversion rates and increase engagement. experiments.title: How including gift array options in an email impacted clicks in a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a sample gift array for a direct email appeal will achieve an increase in engagement because there will be additional cognitive momentum to continue the process to the next step.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2023 - 07/13/2023
In a high urgency campaign, we typically keep the decisions isolated per each step. In the email step, we help the reader decide “Do I want to click?” and then at the donation page step we help the reader decide “How much do I want to give?” In one email, we wanted to test if adding gift array options at the email level would help more people begin thinking about both questions at once. The treatment version added a simple line of copy, letting the reader know that donations of all sizes and amounts were needed and welcomed and would qualify toward the campaign goal: “Whether you give $10, $100, $1,000 or more, every donation counts.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we learned by running this experiment is that adding a sample gift array in the email increased engagement: The treatment version, which included a line of copy mentioning various donation amounts, resulted in a 23.9% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that introducing a gift array at the email level can help in generating additional cognitive momentum and increasing engagement. We also believed this may have motivated more people who were considering making a gift but did not have all of the information needed to move forward with the decision, by reminding them that they could have the autonomy if they wanted to choose to give a gift of $10 or $100 and that all gifts would be counted.
Based on these learnings, future experimentation should consider incorporating sample gift arrays or suggested donation amounts in email appeals. This can potentially encourage donors to consider different giving options and increase their likelihood of clicking on the donation link. Additionally, these findings may also be useful in exploring the impact of dynamic gift arrays in other communication that are based on previous giving history—a tactic often used in direct mail letters that we could test applying in the digital space.

experiments.title: How more user-friendly value proposition copy impacted course sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that more user friendly value proposition copy for course prospects will achieve an increase in email sign up rate because prospects will be more likely to read the full value proposition and will better understand the value that the course will bring to their lives.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2023 - 06/22/2023
DTS is getting a low email conversion rate through their Galatians online course. In reviewing the existing course sign up page, we wonder if the formatting and org-centric messaging is deterring people from signing up for the course. The existing page includes lengthy paragraphs and unusual spacing, and focusing a lot on the organization rather than the prospect.
Therefore, we built a treatment variant that breaks up the lengthy paragraphs into smaller ones, implements typical spacing, and focuses more on what value the course offers to the prospect rather than why the organization created the course. Let’s see which performs better…
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment did not produce a lift in course sign up rate like we anticipated. It led to a 57.9% decrease in course signups with a valid 99.9% level of confidence.
Prospects did not respond well to our simplified, more user-focused value proposition copy. With DTS, it seems the user-centric approach over the org-centric approach is not always best. It may be that brand recognition is strong for DTS and users appreciate how DTS discusses why they created a course and what their overall purpose is.
And maybe despite breaking the two lengthy paragraphs into shorter paragraphs, the fact that there were more paragraphs and overall, and lengthier copy turned away a greater number of users. It may be that users were already motivated to sign up for the course, but seeing multiple paragraphs ahead of the sign up form felt like a barrier to sign up.
Overall, there were multiple elements being tested in this experiment. To gain confident results, it's probably more effective to isolate the elements to determine what specifically is leading to a decrease or an increase.

experiments.title: How increased appeal through value proposition and social proof impacted course sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increased appeal through value proposition and social proof for potential coursetakers will achieve an increase in course sign ups because potential coursetakers will be inspired by what the course offers them and by the overall popularity of the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 06/08/2023
In an effort to increase course sign ups through their Revelations course registration page, DTS has developed a treatment to communicate enhanced appeal for the offer and leverage social proof.
For the treatment, we’re refocusing the value proposition on the potential course taker rather than the organization and the course professor. We want to test the angle of “what can this course do for me” or “what can I get out of this course”. And we also want to leverage social proof to see if joining “one of our most popular courses” or “30,000 people who have already enrolled” will motivate people to take the course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our combination of increased appeal and social proof produced a valid 62.7% decrease in course sign ups during a time span of only 13 days.
It seems this approach was not well-received by our audience, as we saw a decrease in all segments and down-funnel at the donations and revenue levels as well.
Perhaps our audience does appreciate the background of the professor of the course and learning about the organization's purpose for their courses as they get ready to sign up – more so than learning what they may get out of this specific course. Additionally, social proof may not move the needle if someone is interested in the course from a personal education standpoint rather than a "popularity" stand point.
Ultimately, we may want to test different value proposition that better compliments the course instructor and organization's mission instead of taking an entirely different angle like we did in this experiment. We might also test different types of social proof such as testimonials instead of popularity call outs.
We can isolate these elements to get better direction for future tests.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky bar to a year-end fundraising campaign donation page affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to the donation page of a year-end fundraising campaign will increase donation rates by humanizing and further conveying the value of the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2022 - 01/04/2023
During a year-end fundraising campaign for a faith-based education organization, we wanted to test how adding a humanized sticky bar to the donation page would affect donation rates.
We set this experiment up as a redirect test with ~50% of traffic seeing the standard donation page, sans sticky bar, and the other half seeing a humanized sticky bar from one of the organization’s staff members (including a photo) making a personal appeal and expressing the value of the prospect’s donation.
We ran this test for 10 days, during the last stretch of the fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 10 days, the treatment featuring a humanized sticky bar achieved a 14.5% increase in donation rates with an 87.1% statistical level of confidence when compared to the control.
In addition, the treatment including the sticky bar increased revenue per visitor by 151% with a 99% level of confidence, indicating that not only did the sticky bar encourage more people to give, it inspired them to give more generously.
We believe that including a sticky bar featuring a staff member of the organization was able to increase donations among page visitors by humanizing and putting a face to the appeal, reminding people that this organization is comprised of real people, just like them, who are trying to make a difference.
And even though we did not receive a large enough sample size to statistically validate these results, we did observe a directional lift that indicates there is low risk associated with implementing this treatment and we therefore feel confident in recommending the inclusion of a humanized sticky bar to 100% of the traffic during their next high-urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How adding bullets/star bullets to registration page affects sign-up rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a bulleted list of value proposition language for prospective course takers will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2022 - 11/05/2022
DTS offers free online courses using recordings of actual seminary classes. This three-way split test appeared on the Exodus course landing page.
This is a multivariate test. We first wanted to test if adding a list of value proposition language in a highlighted box would better communicate the value that each free course includes and increase conversion rate.
We then wanted to test whether using star emojis as the list’s bullet point icons would attract attention and help communicate value more than normal bullet points, leading to an increase in course signups in all traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, the both treatments produced a small change in conversion rates. The bullet point treatment produced a decrease of 2.2% in course registrations and the star emoji treatment produced a lift of 1.8%, but due to an inadequate sample size, these results did not validate. This makes it difficult to draw a valid conclusion from this experiment.
It's interesting to note that in the email channel, the star emoji treatment produced a valid lift of 13%.
Possible reasons for this increase could be:
the star emoji reminding users of the star emojis seen in an online product review (i.e. Amazon) and signaling the impression of valuethe color and shape of the star emoji attracting the user's attentionthe size of the emoji treatment's container attracting the user's attention moreso than the bullet point treatment's container
As a result, we'll look into rolling this out to email traffic only since the email channel is the main source of traffic for DTS. We'll also look into retesting only the emoji treatment by running it during the launch of a new course to try to get a better sample size and valid results in all traffic channels.

experiments.title: How using a ‘donation interruptor’ affects recurring giving rates during a Giving Tuesday high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that interrupting the donation process with a pop-up for donors on Giving Tuesday will improve recurring gift conversion rates by increasing its visibility at the decision-making point and prominently communicating its value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 12/03/2022
In an effort to increase recurring donor acquisition for a faith-based client during a Giving Tuesday campaign, we wanted to measure the effect of interrupting the donation process with a pop-up asking the donor to become a monthly supporter instead of making only a one-time gift.
This pop-up was set up as an A/B test that would only fire for 50% of donors who were in the process of completing a one-time gift under $100, reiterating the value of becoming a monthly donor both in terms of impact and exclusive benefits.
We tested this same hypothesis with the same client during last year’s Giving Tuesday campaign, and while we saw an encouraging lift in recurring giving, we were unable to validate our results—so we were eager to see how the same concept would perform in a similar setting during this year’s campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Due to an increase in the number of donors (sample size) over last year's campaign, we were pleased to be able to pull a valid learning from this year's experiment—with our treatment producing a statistically valid 137% increase in recurring gifts with a 98.4% level of confidence.
We believe that several motivating factors came into play in producing this result:
First, the interrupter pop-up dramatically increased the visibility of the recurring-giving option. Second, it clearly and thoroughly laid out the value of becoming a recurring donor in terms of impact and exclusive benefitsThird, by creating a mental interruption, it may have given donors an opportunity to reset the mental process of completing their donation (transaction forms being as prevalent as they are in the digital space may cue a certain degree of auto-pilot during the transaction process) and allowed them the time to consider their options.And finally, the pop-up treatment positioned monthly giving as an opportunity rather than an ask, giving agency to the donor and casting them in the role of someone who is able to do (and get) more than they would by making a one-time gift.
We also observed that the treatment produced a statistically valid increase of 210% in recurring giving among donors using mobile devices, important as we continue to see an increase in mobile giving over time.
These results are a positive indication that a similar treatment may work for other clients in the context of high-urgency campaigns and we will seek to globalize this learning by implementing it for new audiences and new giving contexts, such as content offers or direct appeals.

We emphasized what the visitor would get by subscribing instead of what they would have to do
 	We reduced the friction associated with the process by removing copy that we felt was unnecessary to the conversion.

experiments.title: How elaborating on specific organizational initiatives affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an email that elaborates on specific organizational initiatives increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2017 - 12/13/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary recently began their calendar year end campaign, focused three pillars: The Call to Teach, The Will to Engage, and The Heart to Go. As they prepared to send an appeal on email focused on the second pillar–The Will to Engage– they wondered if an email that elaborated on the strategic initiatives they’ve created to engage culture would increase the clickthrough rate to the donation page. They created a treatment version and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
They found that the treatment version did increase clickthrough rate by 276%. It’s clear the treatment version significantly increased recipient motivation, but why? There are a few important elements worth analyzing in the second email. First, the introduction of the treatment version takes a more personal approach to the topic of cultural engagement. It highlights Dr. Bailey’s daily schedule of leaving his office and driving through the city of Dallas. This simple change immediately draws the reader into Dr. Bailey’s point of view and shows why this initiative is personal to him. Humans are storytellers. We love to have a concrete image to hold in our mind’s eye.
Second, the treatment version elaborates on three specific ways Dallas Theological Seminary is addressing culture and empowering individuals outside of the seminary walls. By describing at length these three initiatives–The Table Podcast, the Faith and Work Initiative, and a plethora of free online courses–DTS is reminding their audience where their financial support goes. It’s also important to note the subtle difference in the call-to-action. Instead of clicking to ‘make a secure donation’ this treatment email invites individuals to ‘invest in these initiatives.’ This is an approach that Dallas Theological Seminary will continue to work into the rest of their calendar year end campaign.

experiments.title: How a premium offer affects donor motivation

The treatment with no premium book offer produced a 55.1% increase in donations. Without the “anchor point” of the book, average gift dropped by 34.5%, but there was no discernible impact on revenue.

The fact that the average gift for the control is right at $75 suggests that people either give to get the book, or don’t give at all. This may imply that people are conflating the offer with the incentive, and the aspirational aspect of the value proposition is clouded by the offer.
Because DTS can offer the premium throughout the course (in a banner) and directly through an email, they will move forward with the treatment. As a result of this experiment, DTS was able to grow their donor file significantly faster without affecting revenue.

After visitors registered for the course, they were given the opportunity to give a gift to support the free courses. DTS had traditionally used commentary books as premiums for donations, but wanted to test the impact of removing the premium offer on donations and revenue.
They hypothesized that removing the book might result in more donations, though the average gift might be lower without the “anchor point” ($75, in this case) for the book offer.

experiments.title: How replacing a static image with a video on a Facebook ad affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting an ad in video format will increase click-through rates for Facebook users by better catching their attention and better communicating the value one receives by signing up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2022 - 10/18/2022
Many of Dallas Theological Seminary’s Facebook ads for their free online courses are similar, using a color-rich background including the names of the courses and the teachers who present them. In an effort to combat low click through rates, we hypothesized that although viewers were seeing ads for different courses, because these ads looked so similar, it was causing viewers to assume they were seeing an ad for the same course time and time again, eventually prompting them to tune the ads out.
We also wondered if the static ads were inadequately communicating the value one could receive by enrolling in one of Dallas Theological Seminary’s free online courses. We wanted to know if we could increase click-through rates by presenting a video ad that would 1) Catch viewers attention and 2) Better communicate the value of registration.
For this experiment, we changed only the ad media, splitting traffic between he control and treatment over a span of 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 93% LIFT in click-through rates, with a 100% level of confidence.
This experiment validated our hypothesis that a more interactive media format would result in more viewers clicking the ad. We believe that this treatment was successful for two primary reasons:
First, we believe that the new media format broke up the monotony of similarly formatted ads being shown to the same people. A different ad from a familiar organization, we believe, cued viewers that a new offer was being advertised.
Second, we believe that the video allowed viewers to see for themselves the quality of the coursework and the expertise of the teacher (these courses are adapted from on-campus seminary lectures) which increased the perceived value of the offer by improving its credibility and exclusivity, which likely provided more incentive for the viewer to click.
Moving forward, we will attempt to gather more video samples that we can use to promote more courses in their catalog.

This doesn’t entirely rule out video ads on Facebook. There have been experiments where video ads produce a lift, and they can be effective at remarketing. DTS will continue to test video as a promotional tool for their free courses.

We emphasized what the visitor would get by subscribing instead of what they would have to do
We reduced the friction associated with the process by removing copy that we felt was unnecessary to the conversion.

experiments.title: How a ‘Campaign Opt-Out’ Link Affects Email Unsubscribe Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a ‘Campaign Opt-Out’ link Decrease Email Unsubscribe Rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2017 - 09/13/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary was running a campaign for North Texas Giving Day, a huge fundraising opportunity each year for North Texas nonprofits. As they planned for this campaign, they wanted to give their audience a way to remove themselves from further communication about North Texas Giving Day if they weren’t able to join this year. They wondered: If we allow individuals to ‘opt-out’ of a campaign, will the unsubscribe rate decrease? They created a treatment with one line of copy and a campaign ‘Opt-out’ link for viewers to click, and then they launched an A/B split test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary found that the treatment version decreased the unsubscribe rate by 33%. It’s clear that this treatment reduced the friction and anxiety of the email viewer by offering a clear alternative option for dismissing themselves from this campaign communication. This test shows us that some individuals may not want to unsubscribe from all communication, but when they are left with no other option, they will.
By providing a clear and direct way for viewers to ‘raise their hand’ and decide that they won’t be able to join in a fundraising campaign, we can decrease our email attrition rate and keep a wider audience engaged for a longer period of time with the mission of our organization–in this case, the equipping of Christian leaders.

We were pushing out a Christmas Devotional offer and wanted to re-send to any recipients who had not yet opened or clicked on the original offer to download. The control copy was short and to the point so we wanted to test whether adding additional language—highlighting the value of the resource and emphasizing why this offer would be of particular use to the recipient—would impact clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was the treatment copy actually reduced clicks by 20%. In future experiments, we would want to test including the communal-style language in the body copy while keeping the call to action at the end of the email succinct and clear.

experiments.title: How framing offer content in a Facebook ad impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a Facebook ad that frames the offer content adequately increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/27/2017 - 10/27/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary was promoting their online course Revelation when they noticed an opportunity to test. In their original Facebook course promotion ad, they called on viewers to ‘study with one of DTS’ renowned professors, for free.’ Through previous message testing, they’ve seen that this is a strong value proposition for a lot of their online resources and material. However, they wondered: Will a stronger focus on the book being studied (i.e. Revelation) and its complexity increase motivation and email acquisition rate?
They launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary found that their treatment version did increase email acquisition by 143%. It’s clear that offering more context to the book being studied in the course increased motivation of viewers on Facebook and drove far more course signups then the control version. While the original value proposition remains strong, it was much shorter in length.

This has prompted further message testing that focuses on individual course content, as well as the true value of learning from actual seminary professors for free.

They wondered: If we tell visitors to pre-enroll in this course by stressing the urgency of the upcoming launch date, will email acquisition increase? experiments.title: How refining the donation value proposition affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a value proposition focused on the resource center improve conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2015 - 11/18/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a resource center for biblical perspectives on today’s issues. The goal of this resource center was email and donor acquisition. Immediately after the signup page was a donation ask.
DTS had run a similar value proposition experiment on this page before but had seen no significant difference in results. Taking those learnings, they did a more radical test. They tested which value proposition was stronger: an ask for an investment in a student or an ask supporting the provision of the resource center. They also changed the placement of the endorsement and added a photo of the endorser.

This is also an indicator of the value proposition being reinforced in the messaging and having suggested giving amounts instead of an open field.

experiments.title: How disclosing the amount raised affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will stating progress towards a goal in an email appeal affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2016 - 09/20/2016
This experiment had a limited sample size due to the targeted nature of the experiment. While we were able to reach a statistically significant result, it should be noted that this small number of conversions could be causing sample size distortion and should be tested again with a larger segment of donors.
The treatment copy with the goal increased conversion by 130.8%, which shows that giving donors specific details about the campaign increases the likelihood that they will convert.
There’s another force at work here: social proof. Persuasion science shows that people are more likely to get involved in an activity if they know other people are doing it as well. Disclosing the amount implied that many other donors have already given, and gives the current reader (who has not yet given) the opportunity to be the “closer” on the campaign.
This is a powerful learning that can be employed in many different forms to reduce anxiety and increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary was raising money in honor of North Texas Giving day, and had a group of donors pledge $50,000 if they could raise $50,000 in a week.

They had written an email to existing donors that was very personal in tone, and wanted to test the impact of telling the donors how much had been raised up to that point. They created a treatment email that included a single extra line of copy:

“As of right now, we’ve already raised half of that amount.”

They split the donor file and sent one treatment to each segment to see if they could quantify the impact of this single statement.

experiments.title: How a premium offer affects donor motivation
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the presence of a premium offer affect donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2016 - 11/14/2016
The treatment with no premium book offer produced a 55.1% increase in donations. Without the “anchor point” of the book, average gift dropped by 34.5%, but there was no discernible impact on revenue.
This indicated something deeper about the motivation of their donors: without the premium offer, the value proposition was entirely centered around supporting the free online courses—and more donors responded. This suggests that DTS prospects are more motivated by an aspirational offer rather than a tactile offer.
The fact that the average gift for the control is right at $75 suggests that people either give to get the book, or don’t give at all. This may imply that people are conflating the offer with the incentive, and the aspirational aspect of the value proposition is clouded by the offer.
Because DTS can offer the premium throughout the course (in a banner) and directly through an email, they will move forward with the treatment. As a result of this experiment, DTS was able to grow their donor file significantly faster without affecting revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary had released a free online course on the book of Revelation. After visitors registered for the course, they were given the opportunity to give a gift to support the free courses. DTS had traditionally used commentary books as premiums for donations, but wanted to test the impact of removing the premium offer on donations and revenue.

They hypothesized that removing the book might result in more donations, though the average gift might be lower without the “anchor point” ($75, in this case) for the book offer.

They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How subject line copy that “teases” affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line results in a higher open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/17/2015 - 11/20/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a free online course studying the Gospel of John. They were promoting it by renting email lists, and wanted to determine which subject lines encouraged more recipients to open the email. They developed two subject lines based on hypotheses about the prospect. The first was very direct: “Study the Gospel of John with DTS”. The second teased the offer, and expanded from the acronym: “A special offer from Dallas Theological Seminary”.
experiments.keyLearnings: This tells us that prospects are more likely to open an email when we tease the contents. While clarity has proven to trump persuasion over and over again, the subject line is a different element. It only has one job — to win the open. We can’t get people to click if they don’t open. So this suggests that teasing recipients of this email might increase open rate, which is the metric that affects every other following metric.

experiments.title: How removing creative elements affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removal of creative elements like the graphical banner improve clarity and increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2015 - 12/07/2015
Removing the banner not only decreased conversion by nearly 20%, it also directionally reduced the average gift of those who signed up and then made a donation.
This reminded us that, while clarity is an important factor in the value proposition, appeal and credibility are essential as well. In this case, the graphical elements seem to add credibility to the course.
In addition, while the use of the word “study” might convey work, it also seems to contribute to the credibility of the course, and may align with a key motivation of visitors to the page.
Though other experiment have shown that removing creative elements from the page might improve conversion, this instance proves yet again that what might be a “best practice” for one landing page must be tested on another.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a free online course studying the Gospel of John that was enrolling students at a rapid clip. DTS wanted to see if removing creative elements would improve the clarity of the offer by allowing the eye to focus on the headline, which delivered the value proposition. They also wanted to test a different headline that used another word than “Study”, which conveyed work to the visitor.

So they launched an experiment to determine whether streamlining the page would improve clarity and increase visitor-to-student conversion.

experiments.title: How clarity in the call-to-action affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call-to-action with a compelling video increase course signups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2017 - 02/19/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a new course called “Can You Trust the Bible?” They had shot a video of their Academic Dean explaining the course, and wanted to see how it performed against a direct invitation from the President to join the course. So they created two entrance paths to the course. The first was their control—a direct ask, via email, from the President of DTS. The second was an invitation from the President with a call-to-action to “watch the video to learn more about the course”. They hypothesized that the softer call-to-action would drive more traffic to the landing page, and the short video with a clear call-to-action would produce more overall conversions.
They split their file and closely monitored the data to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 32.6% decrease in signups from the control. This shows that the softer ask does not produce more motivation to sign up for the course, even with the added benefit of the video. There could be a few factors at play here. First, all clicks are not created equal—a click to “learn more”, in this case, is far less valuable than a click to “sign up”. Second, video might actually be obscuring the value proposition by not allowing the user to read it in their own time. Many people can read much faster than a video can communicate information, which leads them to be more likely to accept an offer where they control the cadence of the value proposition.
This does not necessarily mean, however, that the video doesn’t have a place in the marketing schedule of a new course—it may just mean that it needs to be placed later in the schedule to motivate users who were not incentivized by the original message.

experiments.title: How simplifying a cultivation email template impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying an email template for online course takers on the housefile will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2022 - 07/29/2022
When sending a cultivation email offering multiple free courses to the housefile, we developed a control that had hyperlinked images and a course description that included the number of sessions for each course and the name of the instructor. Because we had previously tested into a simplified template with this audience for a monthly newsletter, we also developed a treatment that simply introduced the courses as individual hyperlinks in bullet format. We split the file 50/50 and sent the email to see which version of the email would generate more clicks to the registration page.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was the treatment that had no images led to a 71% increase in click rate with 100% level of confidence, validly generating more traffic to the course registration pages. Interestingly enough, when we followed the control vs. the treatment visitors further downstream, we noticed that overall donations to these 3 courses experienced a 49% decrease for course registrants who came through the treatment version of the email with 98% level of confidence, making it a valid decrease. So although on the surface, it looks like we were able to convert more traffic from the email, we saw inverse results when we analyzed donor conversion rate in this experiment. Our hypothesis is that because the course images and descriptions could potentially add more perceived value in the mind of the course subscriber and better align with the landing page and donation page design experience, the control was able to produce more donations.

A new free online course was taught by the President of the Seminary. We had seen other organizations using evidentials in the form of testimonials to improve email acquisition results and we wanted to test this on the registration page to see if we could increase conversion by adding an element to enhance the visitor’s level of trust and perceived authority of the offer. We built a treatment that included a photo and quote from the online course instructor about what key learnings they would take away from the course by sharing what he personally has learned from studying the book.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the treatment for one month, we saw a 34.7% increase in email conversion rate with 99.9% level of confidence so the results are valid. We found that adding the President's name, likeness, and personal testimonial and affiliation with the course to the body of the acquisition page gave the offer more credibility by providing evidentials to why a person should sign up for the course and increased the likelihood that a visitor would give away their email address to move forward in signing up for the course. We will want to test this concept using other professors for other courses to see if the credibility holds equal weight across other faculty members or if using the Seminary President gave the offer more credibility. We'll find additional opportunities to promote trust and authority to see if this lift can be replicated for multiple course offerings.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and credibility from a relevant leadership figure impacted giving on a main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting a relevant leadership figure for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.

The main donation page for DTS had for a long time featured a quote and photo of their Chancellor and well-known pastor, Chuck Swindoll— included to enhance the Seminary’s credibility in training pastors as people are deciding to make a gift. With a transition in DTS leadership, they had a new Chancellor, Dr. Mark Bailey. Rather than updating the page with a quote and image of Dr. Bailey, we wanted to test into it to see what impact, if any, it had on donations. We also hypothesized whether we should feature the DTS Chancellor or the DTS President, as the President has more current visibility and relevance. We were also curious if featuring someone who was a professor for multiple free online courses would be more recognizable and familiar to page visitors. We left the Chancellor Emeritus quote and photo as the control and developed a series of 3 treatments to test against it. 1) A quote from Chancellor Mark Bailey, 2) A quote from current President Mark Yarbrough about taking the gospel to the world and 3) A quote from Dr Yarbrough about the credentials and qualifications of DTS staff.

The second treatment showed a 69.7% increase in donor conversion with 99% confidence. We believe this is because the page visitors are more familiar with Dr. Yabrough as they often receive emails from him or see him featured on online courses and podcasts and chapel messages. experiments.title: How reducing friction by removing a phone number field impacted gifts

rows.name: 1 Samuel Donation Page - No Phone Number Field

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing form friction by eliminating optional fields for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2022 - 05/14/2022
We were looking for test ideas for an upcoming fiscal year end campaign. We were launching a new free online course ahead of the FYE campaign and knew that we would be getting lots of traffic from sending the offer to the housefile so we wanted to put a test in place on the donation page. We were hoping to get a learning ahead of the fiscal year end campaign that would help us present the most optimized version of the donation form during the campaign. We noticed the donation form fields take up a good amount of space on the page and it looks like a lengthy and difficult process. We wanted to remove the barrier of “work” from our donation page visitors so we asked if there were any unnecessary form fields that could be eliminated as well as any fields that could be grouped together to reduce the amount of space they were taking up on the page. We pulled out phone number and put City/State/Zip all on the same row so it condensed the appearance of the form fields.
experiments.keyLearnings: By taking out the phone number field, even though it was optional and not required, we saw a 77.1% increase in conversion rate with 98.4% level of confidence. Revenue also experienced a 66.8% increase with a 96% level of confidence. We believe removing the phone number field reduced anxiety for page visitors as they didn't have to wonder "Why do you need my phone number in order for me to make a gift?" or "Who is going to contact me and how often if I give away this personal piece of information?"  We were also able to reduce friction by making the form appear shorter and look like less work for the donor when filling out the form. Going forward into the FYE campaign, we will remove the phone number field on the donation form and find ways to ask for it after the gift is made rather than interrupting the donation process to get this information.

We will test again using a less definite question and instead choose one that invokes curiosity and we will test using imagery that is more distinctly varied from the control to draw attention and combat creative fatigue.

experiments.title: How using donor-centric language in a calendar year end appeal impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using donor-centric language for a calendar year end appeal email will achieve a higher click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2021 - 12/08/2021
As we reviewed copy for the year end campaign, we realized the direct mail messaging for the campaign was very organization-centric and not at all focused on the donor or their impact. A single letter referenced the organization “DTS” 12 times and contained the word “We” another 10 times while only using the word “you” twice. We decided to test how this message would compare to a more personalized appeal that used donor-centric language. The treatment presented the same information and value proposition for why a donor should give, but it was heavily revised to focus on the donor rather than the accomplishments of the organization. The treatment copy reduced “DTS” references to just four mentions and used one-to-one communication including the word “I” four times and “you/your” 10 times.
experiments.keyLearnings: We actually had the opportunity to test this concept twice. The first experiment only reached 45% level of confidence that there was any lift in traffic, but on the second send to recipients who had not opened the first email, we saw an 85% increase in traffic to the donation page with 99% confidence. The donor-centric language motivated more visitors to take the next step toward making a gift. We will want to test this approach again during the next high urgency campaign. Once they arrived on the donation page, we measured instant conversion rate there as well and did not achieve a valid level of confidence that there was any lift in donations from the segment who arrived on the page from the treatment version of the email. The learnings from this test could be applied and tested in the next direct mail appeal as well to see how donor-centric language performs in another channel.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Do suggested gift amounts convert better than an open field?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2015 - 12/07/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a free online course to grow their email file and attract new donors. After a visitor signed up for the course, they were presented with an opportunity to give, accompanied by a premium offer to receive a Bible commentary.
Previous testing and optimization with DTS had shown that their donors preferred an open field instead of a gift array with recommended gift amounts. But as this course began to take off, they realized they were bringing new people to the page. They hypothesized that these new people might benefit from a choice of gift options rather than a blank field.
So they created a second page with the same copy, but three gift options above the donation form and ran an A/B split test to find out which one resulted in more conversions and donation revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the gift array produced a 34.9% lift in conversion. However, it also produced a 21% decrease in average gift. Since the decrease in average gift was less than the increase in conversion, the treatment also generated more revenue, which made it the winner.
This tells us that the “unlimited choice” of an open field produces too much decision friction in this instance. It also reminds us that as an audience grows past those who are familiar with an organization, previously tested ideas might be called into question — which reinforces the value and importance of continual testing and optimization.

experiments.title: How impression-based advertising to donor audiences impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing for impressions for Wiland-modeled audiences will achieve improved cost per donor results.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/20/2021 - 09/01/2021
DTS had been promoting their online course, Jonah, via Facebook to several different audience groups. One of those segments was a set of modeled audiences from Wiland. This included the specifically modeled donors and donor lookalikes generated on Facebook based upon the Wiland segments. We had seen declining results from these groups so we wanted to test different tactics to get more out of these prime audiences.
It had been suggested to us that since these were identified donor prospects, we should change our advertising optimization metrics from “Purchases” (aka donations) to be based upon “Reach”. The hypothesis was that showing the offer to more prospective donors would reduce ad costs while improving results. We decided to test this hypothesis against the standard Purchase optimization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After spending $2,500 on each of the two campaigns, we found that the Purchase optimized campaign resulted in 26 new donors while the Reach optimized campaign received zero gifts. This is similar to the kind of results we see with donor lookalike audiences generated by Facebook when we use "Reach" as the optimization metrics.
It should be noted that both the "Reach" and "Purchase" campaigns spent a greater proportion of budget on the Gold and Platinum lookalike audiences. However, the native Wiland audiences received several hundred in spend each while returning zero donors for the Reach campaign.

As we significantly increased media budget on Facebook ads, we wanted to run treatment versions of existing free online course ads to optimize our spend and avoid creative fatigue for the digital audiences we were targeting. We also increased clarity in the offer by outlining what someone could expect: “Through this course, you’ll find encouragement in God’s Word to deal with daily issues with an eternal mindset.” For the second treatment, we kept the copy the same as the control and simply used a graphic of a Bible verse relevant to the course topic to demonstrate alignment of values and identity.

The treatment that had copy changes only did not validate for donor conversion, and actually decreased the click rate to .5% with 100% level of confidence. The next iteration of the test will be to test a version of the ad with the treatment copy paired with the treatment image so that the concept of identity alignment is present in both elements. We are also testing similar graphics on other online course ads to see if these results are consistent across all offers.

experiments.title: How a relevant subject line affected open rates for a newsletter

rows.name: Russell, I wanted to encourage you today

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding relevancy for a housefile newsletter will achieve increased email engagment.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2021 - 05/03/2021
DTS introduced a new sender for their monthly newsletter and wanted to test how the file would respond to different subject lines. We set up a highly personalized control “Kevin, I wanted to encourage you today” and a highly relevant yet mysterious treatment “Happy Friday!” Neither the control nor the treatment gives away the contents of the email, but both subject lines indicate the message is both authentic from the sender and specifically for you. The treatment adds relevancy by including a day of the week and we wanted to see how this influenced email open rate so we ran a full A/B split test on the monthly newsletter send.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found in our A/B test was that the "Happy Friday" subject line did slightly increase the open rate by 1.3% with 98.7% confidence. However, we also analyzed the click rate of the newsletter, which experienced a 13.9% decrease with a 100% level of confidence. Taking this into consideration, moving forward we will continue to use personalization and mystery in subject lines to maintain and improve overall email engagement. We want the monthly newsletter recipients to not just open the email but to also engage with it and receive value from the contents included. Seeing the small bump in open rate is not nearly as valuable when we simultaneously saw such a decline on the treatment clickthrough rate. Looking ahead, we'd rather have the file be more deeply engaged in a way that they are taking that next step to demonstrate they are interested, connected and invested than to have a 1.3% improvement on the open rate for future newsletters. This is why it's important to fully measure our results.

experiments.title: How adding benefits of monthly giving in an email impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including benefits of monthly giving in both the email copy and donation page copy increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/23/2020
DTS was leading a campaign to acquire new monthly donors for our Cornerstone Partners monthly giving program. The donation page clearly listed the benefits of becoming a Cornerstone Partner and we wondered if including these same benefits directly in the email appeal would influence the number of gifts received. The hypothesis was that presenting potential donors with the information in the body copy of the email would lead to more highly motivated traffic on the donation page and increase conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The control copy explained why monthly giving was of benefit to the organization, but the treatment copy introduced why monthly giving would be of value to the individual donor and how they would personally benefit from making a monthly rather than a one-time gift. By introducing this clarity in perceived value, we were able to increase donor conversion rate by 122.7%. In addition to listing the benefits, the treatment copy also included a sentence clearly communicating that a donor's monthly giving commitment could be altered or suspended as needed in the future. experiments.title: How copy and a new image can increase conversions in a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a redesigned ad that reinforces value prop and adds clarity and urgency to the call to action increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/27/2018
DTS was relaunching their Can You Trust the Bible course and wanted to increase conversions from the Facebook ad. They decided to test a new body copy treatment as well as a new image with larger text and a new image in order to motivate more people to get the free course.
Their hypothesis for testing was that people were not connecting with the value proposition of defending the inerrancy of the Bible because the copy did not call people into action, or use scriptural references to reiterate the importance of being able to defend the Bible and its truth amidst a culture that rejects its validity.
The treatment ad focused on the reader taking action in learning to defend the Bible through the online course, and also utilized an image that was more closely tied to the value proposition of the copy to increase the strength of the value prop and add clarity.
The headline also made a difference, with a headline that added urgency and a more direct call to action- “Get the free course now” versus “learn more about it.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new treatment increased conversion by 26%. This shows us that more people connected with the value prop of the new ad versus the original. 

This is because the control copy focused on doubt surrounding the inerrancy of the Bible and how the course would help Christians defend the word of God, while the treatment copy encouraged readers to not only be able to defend the Bible, but to equip themselves with its truth in order effectively communicate their beliefs.  

The treatment image also included someone reading the Bible which reinforced the value proposition that the answers to being able to defend the Bible are located within it. The new call to action added urgency (Get it now) and reinforced that the course was free. The treatment image also had enlarged copy, which also increased clarity.

A combination of all of these elements led to a higher motivation for the reader.

This tells us that the “unlimited choice” of an open field produces too much decision friction in this instance. It also reminds us that as an audience grows past those who are familiar with an organization, previously tested ideas might be called into question — which reinforces the value and importance of continual testing and optimization.

experiments.title: How disclosing the amount raised affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will stating progress towards a goal in an email appeal affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2016 - 09/20/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary was raising money in honor of North Texas Giving day, and had a group of donors pledge $50,000 if they could raise $50,000 in a week.
They had written an email to existing donors that was very personal in tone, and wanted to test the impact of telling the donors how much had been raised up to that point. They created a treatment email that included a single extra line of copy:
“As of right now, we’ve already raised half of that amount.”
They split the donor file and sent one treatment to each segment to see if they could quantify the impact of this single statement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had a limited sample size due to the targeted nature of the experiment. While we were able to reach a statistically significant result, it should be noted that this small number of conversions could be causing sample size distortion and should be tested again with a larger segment of donors.
The treatment copy with the goal increased conversion by 130.8%, which shows that giving donors specific details about the campaign increases the likelihood that they will convert.
There’s another force at work here: social proof. Persuasion science shows that people are more likely to get involved in an activity if they know other people are doing it as well. Disclosing the amount implied that many other donors have already given, and gives the current reader (who has not yet given) the opportunity to be the “closer” on the campaign.
This is a powerful learning that can be employed in many different forms to reduce anxiety and increase conversion.

experiments.title: How a premium offer affects donor motivation
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the presence of a premium offer affect donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2016 - 11/14/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary had released a free online course on the book of Revelation. After visitors registered for the course, they were given the opportunity to give a gift to support the free courses. DTS had traditionally used commentary books as premiums for donations, but wanted to test the impact of removing the premium offer on donations and revenue.
They hypothesized that removing the book might result in more donations, though the average gift might be lower without the “anchor point” ($75, in this case) for the book offer.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with no premium book offer produced a 55.1% increase in donations. Without the “anchor point” of the book, average gift dropped by 34.5%, but there was no discernible impact on revenue.
This indicated something deeper about the motivation of their donors: without the premium offer, the value proposition was entirely centered around supporting the free online courses—and more donors responded. This suggests that DTS prospects are more motivated by an aspirational offer rather than a tactile offer.
The fact that the average gift for the control is right at $75 suggests that people either give to get the book, or don’t give at all. This may imply that people are conflating the offer with the incentive, and the aspirational aspect of the value proposition is clouded by the offer.
Because DTS can offer the premium throughout the course (in a banner) and directly through an email, they will move forward with the treatment. As a result of this experiment, DTS was able to grow their donor file significantly faster without affecting revenue.

experiments.title: How subject line personalization affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does subject line personalization affect open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2016 - 06/15/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary was promoting their annual Christmas devotional series to their house email file. In the past, they had used a very straightforward subject line to promote the offer: Devotionals for the Christmas Season. But their new marketing platform allowed them to personalize the subject line with the recipient’s firstname, so they put it to a test.
They sent 10% of their house file each treatment and collected data to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the personalized subject line produced a 22.5% lift in open rate, which showed that including the recipient’s first name in the subject line increases open rate.

But the personalization wasn’t the only factor—the treatment subject line also obscured the actual payoff of the email by not disclosing the offer. This appeared to heighten interest in what the “reveal” would be, intensified by the personalization factor.

The combination of these two factors led to a significant increase in open rate, which helped inform future subject line copy. However, the “trick” of personalization also may come with a significant diminishing return if overused.

Since DTS got all the home address data when they made a gift, they didn’t need to collect it at the beginning.
They were curious how this might affect course registrations, so they launched a test between a full signup form and one that just asked for name, email, and password. They split the traffic to determine the effect of removing the home address fields.

The form is now reduced to the bare essentials of what data must be collected for the course registration, so more value proposition testing is prompted.

experiments.title: How clarity in the call-to-action affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call-to-action with a compelling video increase course signups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2017 - 02/19/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a new course called “Can You Trust the Bible?” They had shot a video of their Academic Dean explaining the course, and wanted to see how it performed against a direct invitation from the President to join the course. So they created two entrance paths to the course. The first was their control—a direct ask, via email, from the President of DTS. The second was an invitation from the President with a call-to-action to “watch the video to learn more about the course”. They hypothesized that the softer call-to-action would drive more traffic to the landing page, and the short video with a clear call-to-action would produce more overall conversions.
They split their file and closely monitored the data to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 32.6% decrease in signups from the control. This shows that the softer ask does not produce more motivation to sign up for the course, even with the added benefit of the video. There could be a few factors at play here. First, all clicks are not created equal—a click to “learn more”, in this case, is far less valuable than a click to “sign up”. Second, video might actually be obscuring the value proposition by not allowing the user to read it in their own time. Many people can read much faster than a video can communicate information, which leads them to be more likely to accept an offer where they control the cadence of the value proposition.
This does not necessarily mean, however, that the video doesn’t have a place in the marketing schedule of a new course—it may just mean that it needs to be placed later in the schedule to motivate users who were not incentivized by the original message.

They split audiences and tested each ad unit to see which resulted in a better cost-per-acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The video ads produced a 37% decrease in conversion when tested against the static ads. While a deeper look at the data showed that many more people engaged with the video ads (which means they clicked somewhere on the ad), the video ad was either too distracting or not compelling enough to convince them to take the next step and sign up for the course. This seems to indicate that, in this case, video is more of a distraction than a motivator to take the course.
This doesn’t entirely rule out video ads on Facebook. There have been experiments where video ads produce a lift, and they can be effective at remarketing. DTS will continue to test video as a promotional tool for their free courses.

experiments.title: How a value proposition built upon “what” instead of “why” impacts email acquisition
