Dallas Theological Seminary had recently launched their first online course, a study of the Gospel of John. The course was offered for free in exchange for a visitor’s name, email, and address. With significant traffic being sent to the site, we wanted to find the best way to communicate the value to the visitors.
Using the learnings from experiments run with similar organization, we tried applying two key principles to the copy on the page:
We emphasized what the visitor would get by subscribing instead of what they would have to do
We reduced the friction associated with the process by removing copy that we felt was unnecessary to the conversion.
We then tested these two pages against each other and monitored results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment ended up decreasing email acquisition by 6%. Our hypothesis is that this was not caused by reducing friction or emphasizing the value to the visitor but because we removed the “why” behind the course and focused solely on the “what.”
The paragraph removed talked about the mission of DTS and it allowed visitors to the page to align around this same mission and mentally put themselves in the same audience. Those that felt they belonged would be more likely to take the desired action of signing up.

experiments.title: How a ‘Campaign Opt-Out’ Link Affects Email Unsubscribe Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a ‘Campaign Opt-Out’ link Decrease Email Unsubscribe Rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2017 - 09/13/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary was running a campaign for North Texas Giving Day, a huge fundraising opportunity each year for North Texas nonprofits. As they planned for this campaign, they wanted to give their audience a way to remove themselves from further communication about North Texas Giving Day if they weren’t able to join this year. They wondered: If we allow individuals to ‘opt-out’ of a campaign, will the unsubscribe rate decrease? They created a treatment with one line of copy and a campaign ‘Opt-out’ link for viewers to click, and then they launched an A/B split test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary found that the treatment version decreased the unsubscribe rate by 33%. It’s clear that this treatment reduced the friction and anxiety of the email viewer by offering a clear alternative option for dismissing themselves from this campaign communication. This test shows us that some individuals may not want to unsubscribe from all communication, but when they are left with no other option, they will.
By providing a clear and direct way for viewers to ‘raise their hand’ and decide that they won’t be able to join in a fundraising campaign, we can decrease our email attrition rate and keep a wider audience engaged for a longer period of time with the mission of our organization–in this case, the equipping of Christian leaders.

They launched an A/B test leading up to their launch date to find out.

Dallas Theological Seminary found that the treatment version that stressed the upcoming launch date did, in fact, increase email acquisition by 88%. By using the actual date of the launch (October 16, 2017) and the word ‘Monday’ in their call-to-action, Dallas Theological Seminary was able to signal to viewers that this was an urgent offer with a specific launch date. It likely made viewers feel that they were going to miss out on a particular event, even though the course was still offered after the launch date.
This strategy has prompted further tests, especially as Dallas Theological Seminary builds plans to launch new courses.

It’s clear that offering more context to the book being studied in the course increased motivation of viewers on Facebook and drove far more course signups then the control version. While the original value proposition remains strong, it was much shorter in length.
This has prompted further message testing that focuses on individual course content, as well as the true value of learning from actual seminary professors for free.

experiments.title: How elaborating on specific organizational initiatives affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an email that elaborates on specific organizational initiatives increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2017 - 12/13/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary recently began their calendar year end campaign, focused three pillars: The Call to Teach, The Will to Engage, and The Heart to Go. As they prepared to send an appeal on email focused on the second pillar–The Will to Engage– they wondered if an email that elaborated on the strategic initiatives they’ve created to engage culture would increase the clickthrough rate to the donation page. They created a treatment version and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
They found that the treatment version did increase clickthrough rate by 276%. It’s clear the treatment version significantly increased recipient motivation, but why? There a few important elements worth analyzing in the second email. First, the introduction of the treatment version takes a more personal approach to the topic of cultural engagement. It highlights Dr. Bailey’s daily schedule of leaving his office and driving through the city of Dallas. This simple change immediately draws the reader into Dr. Bailey’s point of view and shows why this initiative is personal to him. Humans are storytellers. We love to have a concrete image to hold in our mind’s eye.
Second, the treatment version elaborates on three specific ways Dallas Theological Seminary is addressing culture and empowering individuals outside of the seminary walls. By describing at length these three initiatives–The Table Podcast, the Faith and Work Initiative, and a plethora of free online courses–DTS is reminding their audience where their financial support goes. It’s also important to note the subtle difference in the call-to-action. Instead of clicking to ‘make a secure donation’ this treatment email invites individuals to ‘invest in these initiatives.’ This is an approach that Dallas Theological Seminary will continue to work into the rest of their calendar year end campaign.

experiments.title: How a multiple choice mission-focused question on an email affects the donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Using a support directed, mission-focused multiple choice question during a calendar year-end campaign will increase the conversion rate because they will read about the many ways the organization works towards their mission and the resources needed to accomplish them all.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/16/2025
Dallas Theological Seminary wants to reach more people worldwide with biblical-truth and teach people how to study the Bible.
We ran this test during their calendar year-end campaign. Traffic came to this page through email. We sent the email to everyone on their house file.
We wanted to know if more people would donate after presented with four mission-focused and specific answers. The control email had a yes or no question, focusing only on one aspect of the DTS mission.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
1. The use of a support-directed, mission-focused multiple choice question during a calendar year-end campaign resulted in a significant increase in donations for Dallas Theological Seminary.
2. The treatment group, which was presented with four mission-focused and specific answers, experienced a 137.9% increase in donations compared to the control group.
3. The statistical level of confidence was 92.8%, indicating that the results are approaching validity but further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the treatment.
Future Implications:
1. Incorporating multiple mission-focused options in donation appeals can potentially improve the effectiveness of fundraising campaigns.
2. Future experimentation should continue to explore different ways of presenting the organization's mission and impact to donors to maximize donation outcomes.
3. Conducting additional tests with larger sample sizes and varied messaging strategies can help validate the initial findings and provide more robust insights for future campaign optimization.

experiments.title: How a skip the donate page button impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “skip to my course” option for instant donation page viewers will achieve the same amount of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/13/2024 - 09/10/2024
DTS offers free online courses as content offers. When somebody signs up for a free course, they then go to a instant donation page. This has causes some course enrollees to get confused and email DTS asking why they need to pay for a free course. We decided to do an A/B/C test and test putting “You can access your course immediately by clicking here.” in the copy and adding a button to the bottom of the page that says “No thanks, take me to my course now.” We wanted to see if adding clarity would impact donations and impact the amount of people emailing DTS.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a "skip to my course" option on the instant donation page did not lead to the same amount of donations as the control group. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions for all traffic. While the results are not statistically significant at the 95% level, they are approaching validity at 86.9%. This suggests that further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact on donations.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and design of the instant donation page. While clarity is crucial in guiding users to their desired action, such as accessing their course immediately, it is also important to balance this with the donation call-to-action. It may be beneficial to test different variations of the messaging and design to find the optimal combination that drives both course access and donations.
Overall, the results from this experiment provide valuable insights for future testing and optimization of the instant donation page to improve user experience and donation conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a banner style Facebook ad impacted clicks to the donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that running a yes/no ad during a high urgency campaign for a direct mail audience will achieve increased clicks to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/10/2021
During a high urgency campaign, we wanted to target direct mail recipients with digital ads to prime and reinforce the campaign messaging in order to drive additional highly motivated and qualified traffic to the donation page. We wanted to test our control copy which included the deadline and the Challenge Gift against a treatment that made a specific ask to give a monthly gift and become a new Cornerstone Partner. We also developed a second treatment that asked a question to frame and align the donor’s values and beliefs with the organization and used more sliding door copy that presents what happens if we do not equip Christian leaders with Scripture. We wanted to activate the power of the “micro-yes” through this ad and bring those visitors to the donation page while they are already nodding their heads in agreement with the mission of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: While neither treatment had enough run-time to see valid results since it was a short, time-bound campaign, the ad that had "Learn more about becoming a Cornerstone Partner" as the call to action increased traffic by 65.6% with a 91.8% level of confidence. The second treatment with the banner graphic also drove clicks up 21.8% but only had a 62.7% level of confidence. We will look for additional opportunities to test the banner graphic in acquisition ads to see how the belief alignment impacts traffic and conversion rate for both online courses and instant donation pages.

experiments.title: How using donor-centric language impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing organizational language for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there is more “you” focused language that aligns the donor’s beliefs with the donation ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2024 - 06/14/2024
When sending visitors from a free course registration page to a donation form, we noticed there were multiple references to the organization and fewer references to the page visitor. We wondered if treating this donation page like a conversation with a potential donor would improve conversion on the page. We developed a treatment based on a past variant that had out-performed the control and ran the test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing organizational language for donation page visitors can potentially increase donor conversion. The treatment, which involved using more "you" focused language to align the donor's beliefs with the donation ask, resulted in a 20.6% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 82.7%, so the results are not statistically valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue exploring the impact of using donor-centric language on donation page performance. By refining the treatment and running further tests with a larger sample size, we may be able to validate the initial findings and gain more insights into what drives donor conversion on the page.

experiments.title: How using donor autonomy in a CTA impacted clicks on a survey
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving survey takers s a choice rather than a command for housefile email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there is an improved sense of autonomy in the process, giving the potential donors ownership.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/18/2024 - 05/20/2024
The concept behind donor autonomy is that people like to make choices for themselves, rather than being told what to do. When applying this concept to online behavior, we wondered if using language in a CTA that gave the email recipients the chance to indicate their willingness to participate, rather than instructing them with a command would have any influence on the click rate to complete a survey. We split traffic 50/50 between each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that giving survey takers a choice, rather than instructing them with a command, resulted in a 5.9% increase in clicks for all traffic. This suggests that there is a positive impact on engagement when potential donors feel a sense of autonomy and ownership in the process. However, the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, indicating that further testing would be necessary to validate these results.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of language that offer choices to recipients rather than giving commands. In our next test, we will build on the concept of donor autonomy by including the option to say "no" rather than only including one place to click and say "yes" to the request, which may lead to an even greater sense of agency for the email recipient and potentially increased engagement.

experiments.title: How changing the color of form donate buttons impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the color of the donate button for donation forms will achieve a decrease in donor conversion because the color is darker than the previous and is more difficult to read.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/19/2024 - 02/21/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary is celebrating their centennial this year and for it, they had an update to their brand.
For this test, we wanted to see if the updated colors would impact overall donor conversion due to the new yellow/gold being darker than the previous.
We ran this test across all donation pages, including acquisition pages and the main donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The updated color of the donate button for donation forms resulted in a 9.9% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic.

2. Although the increase in One-Time Donations was statistically significant at 81.4% confidence level, it did not meet the standard threshold of 95% confidence for valid results.

3. While the darker color of the donate button may have initially been thought to be more difficult to read and potentially decrease donor conversion, the results showed a positive impact on conversions and revenue.

In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing variations in colors, designs, and elements on donation forms to understand their impact on donor conversion and revenue. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are run for a sufficient duration to gather reliable data and reach a higher level of statistical confidence before making conclusive decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a continuation headline for visitors will achieve an increase in our donor conversion rate because it will provide clarity on their next steps and encourage them to continue reading down the page. because  it will provide clarity on their next steps and encourage them to continue reading down the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 01/05/2024
In an effort to increase our instant donor conversion rate on our Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run a continuation headline test. The new headline reads: “Your 18-month calendar will be mailed to your address shortly. Before you go, would you consider something?”
The continuation headline will help to encourage visitors to read further down the page, enabling them to read more value proposition, and then finally see the donation page.
By removing “Thank you” from the headline, we believe less people will perceive the donation page as the final step in the process and rather, make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a continuation headline on the Prayer Calendar funnel led to a significant increase in revenue for all traffic at a 73.8% lift. This result suggests that the continuation headline effectively communicated the next steps to the visitors and avoided the perception that their contribution was complete.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is necessary to validate the findings and ensure that the continuation headline consistently generates positive results.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the continuation headline to gather more data and increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be valuable to explore other variations in the headline to identify the most effective messaging for visitor conversion.
Overall, the findings of this experiment provide valuable insights for optimizing the Prayer Calendar funnel, but further testing and refinement are required to cement the effectiveness of the continuation headline.

experiments.title: How a more generalized ask in a sticky bar for Giving Tuesday impacted click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing the message in terms of a broader impact will resonate with more people and lead to a higher click-through rate because it is more emotionally engaging.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 11/28/2023
On Giving Tuesday, Dallas Theological Seminary made a concerted effort to acquire more monthly donors, called Cornerstone Partners. They’ve typically taken an approach that outlines the number of new Cornerstone Partners needed before the midnight deadline.
This year, they wanted to see if a more general ask would lead to higher click-through rates because the goal number could be perceived as overwhelming for someone reading.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment version of the message, which framed the ask in terms of a broader impact, did not lead to a higher click-through rate compared to the control version. In fact, the treatment version saw a significant decrease in clicks by 61.1% with a 93.1% level of confidence.
These results suggest that the hypothesis was incorrect and that framing the message in terms of a broader impact may not resonate with more people or be more emotionally engaging. Therefore, it may not be an effective strategy for increasing click-through rates and acquiring more monthly donors.
In future experimentation, it would be necessary to further test different messaging strategies to determine what resonates most with the target audience. It is important to take into account the specific characteristics and motivations of the donors to understand what messaging would be most compelling to them.
Additionally, it would be advisable to conduct experiments with larger sample sizes and run the experiment for a longer period to increase the statistical validity of the results. This would help ensure that the findings are more reliable and generalizable.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing different messaging approaches and continuously refining strategies to optimize donor engagement and acquisition.

experiments.title: How unique value propositions impacted clicks for a direct ask sticky bar
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the giving motivation of equipping the next generation of Christian leaders for website visitors will achieve an increase in click conversions because that value will resonate the strongest with our audience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 06/30/2023
DTS is amid their fiscal year end fundraising campaign. With 15 days remaining, we want to add a website sticky bar as an additional revenue opportunity. We’ve built two different sticky bars unique in value proposition. The control uses the value proposition of “reaching lives across the globe with the truth of the Bible” and the control used the value proposition of “equipping the next generation of Christian leaders.” We want to see which value resonates the strongest with our website audience and generates the most clicks to the FYE donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment sticky bar resulted in a 33.3% decrease in clicks with a nearly valid 90.6% level of confidence. The treatment produced 22 clicks compared to 52 to the control.
We noticed the treatment heading in the wrong direction so we ended the experiment after 11 days in order to send 100% of traffic to the control variant.
It seems the control value proposition of "reaching lives across the globe with the truth of the Bible" resonates the most with our online audience. There are other factors to consider with the experiment, however.
The treatment led with "SPECIAL OPPORTUNITY" while the control led with "DEADLINE APPROACHING". Perhaps the control was more effective in this case as it notified users of an important deadline immediately. The treatment lead-in is much more broad which could lead users to overlook it or move casually past it. Also worth noting is the treatment mentioned the Challenge Gift amount of $600,000 while the control only referenced the Challenge Gift without stating the amount.
It's possible that including the $600k amount turned visitors off as they may have felt as if their modest gift would not make a significant difference in the overall goal.
These are all isolated elements that would be worth testing into to get more refined results. Though, in this high urgency scenario, we wanted to act fast and get our best two sticky bars copy versions out to the public.

experiments.title: How removing organization-centric language impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing organization-centric language for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because we speak about the donor and what they believe and can accomplish rather than ourselves. because  we speak about the donor and what they believe and can accomplish rather than ourselves.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/23/2023 - 03/26/2023
When reviewing performance on an instant donation page, we noticed there were several organization-centric references throughout the copy. We wondered if replacing the phrases where we talk about our organization and what we believe with language that instead spoke about the donor using “you” focused messaging could help us achieve a lift in donor response. We split traffic to each version 50/50 and ran the experiment for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month, we observed a 39% lift on the treatment with 90% level of confidence so the experiment did not validate, but we were approaching statistical significance. We believe these results demonstrate that reducing organization-centric language for donation page visitors can lead to an increase in conversion rate and revenue. The 39.4% increase in donations for all traffic and a 556% increase in revenue further support this hypothesis. In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the effectiveness of donor-centric messaging and consider incorporating it in our donation pages.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar impacted donations after a course sign up
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar encouraging support for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the personalized message from a prominent and relatable figure will inspire them to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2023 - 03/12/2023
Dallas Theological Seminary’s Galatians course reroutes users to a donation page after they sign up for the course. This donation page is experiencing a lower than expected donation conversion rate, so we want to test adding a humanized sticky bar to the top of the donation page in hopes that it will effectively nudge more people to make a donation.
The sticky bar messaging includes a thank you for signing up and kindly asks the page visitor to stick around to view a special message about how they can help more people get access to free bible study courses like the one they just signed up for. It also includes a picture and a signature of the organization president and course instructor implying that the message is being stated by that person.
We’ve seen encouraging results for the humanized sticky bar for various organizations so we want to see if it is successful in this instance in increasing donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a 95.5% lift in donations at an 84.1% level of confidence. The results confirm our hypothesis that the personalized message from a prominent and relatable figure would inspire visitors to donate and increase donor conversion rate. It is also important to consider the person speaking through the humanized sticky bar. In this case, users likely appreciate that the message is coming not only from the president of the organization but also the course instructor - the person they will be engaging with as they learn more about Galatians.
The key learning from this experiment is that creating a personalized and humanized experience for potential donors can significantly increase donor conversion rates. The sticky bar separates the page from standard pages - the sticky bar stands out as a unique feature that catches the users attention right away and amplifies the donation value proposition. This treatment should be tested with other course donation pages.

experiments.title: How making an ask for a specific program impacted donation page results – Part II
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a program-specific value proposition for free online course takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2022 - 03/06/2022
As we looked to increase donor conversion for free online courses, we wanted to find what messaging would most resonate with donors. The existing donation page standard copy was centered on the value proposition of getting the free course into the hands of more people. We wanted to test if transitioning the value proposition to a more specific outcome would motivate donation page visitors. We developed the first treatment to focus on using their gift to promote free online courses to pastors to supplement their training and meet this specific need/problem that exists. We developed a second treatment that highlighted current efforts to translate the free online courses into additional languages to expand their reach, which will require additional funding to build and promote. We wanted to find out if either of these treatments would increase donor conversion by addressing the needs at hand the organization is trying to meet and communicating a specific impact that the donor’s gift would be supporting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the split test across all 3 versions for one month on the donation page. What we found was the first treatment that emphasized pastoral training was decreasing results by 58% with 82% level of confidence. On the second treatment however, we learned that the translation of courses seemed to be lifting donor conversion and at the end of the experiment resulted in a 26% directional increase but only had 36% level of confidence. Because we saw similar results on two additional online course donation pages that were running concurrently, we made the decision to roll out the second treatment as the new control for this donation page experience. We will continue to find opportunities to optimize this copy in future testing. Further analysis on individual transactions will be needed to analyze and uncover the cause for the decrease in average gift and revenue.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 82.4% decrease with a 98% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 70.4% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 54.7% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 4.9% increase in donations with an 85% level of confidence.Direct visitors had a 35.7% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 95% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How making an ask for a specific program impacted donation page results
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a program-specific value proposition for free online course takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2022 - 03/06/2022
As we looked to increase donor conversion for free online courses, we wanted to find what messaging would most resonate with donors. The existing donation page standard copy was centered on the value proposition of getting the free course into the hands of more people. We wanted to test if transitioning the value proposition to a more specific outcome would motivate donation page visitors. We developed the first treatment to focus on using their gift to promote free online courses to pastors to supplement their training and meet this specific need/problem that exists. We developed a second treatment that highlighted current efforts to translate the free online courses into additional languages to expand their reach, which will require additional funding to build and promote. We wanted to find out if either of these treatments would increase donor conversion by addressing the needs at hand the organization is trying to meet and communicating a specific impact that the donor’s gift would be supporting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the split test across all 3 versions for one month on the donation page. What we found was the first treatment that emphasized pastoral training was mostly flat and saw a 1% decrease with 2.98% level of confidence. On the second treatment however, we learned that the translation of courses seemed to be lifting donor conversion and at the end of the experiment resulted in a 68% directional increase with 89% level of confidence. Because we saw similar results on two additional online course donation pages that were running concurrently, we made the decision to roll out the second treatment as the new control for this donation page experience. We will continue to find opportunities to optimize this copy in future testing.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Paid Social visitors had a 106.9% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.New visitors had a 101.8% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 154.0% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 400.0% increase in donations with a 90% level of confidence.Direct visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation page impacted monthly and one-time gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign focused on recurring giving for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in monthly recurring donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2021 - 01/07/2022
Ahead of their calendar year-end campaign, DTS sent out a digital Christmas Devotional as a cultivation piece to their housefile. The control donation page used their standard appeal language with a paragraph outlining the benefits of monthly giving. To increase recurring gifts, we wanted to test a donation page treatment that specifically asked for a monthly recurring gift emphasizing a microask goal of 10 new daily monthly donors. In addition to the microask goal, we made several other optimizations to the page. We swapped out the headline to reflect that their monthly gift would have an impact 365 days a year and introduced the concept of donor autonomy and value alignment throughout the copy by asking conversational questions such as “Would you consider becoming a Cornerstone Partner?” and “Will you make a monthly gift today to help share biblical truth with the world?” We split traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment for visitors after they signed up to receive their copy of the digital Christmas Devotional.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not receive enough monthly gifts to validate the experiment's impact on recurring giving, we did see other interesting trends in overall giving as a result of running the test. We did not reach statistical significance overall, but we identified that the treatment page was showing a 29.4% lift in donor conversion with 86% level of confidence. Furthermore, as you'll see below, when we analyzed traffic by channel, mobile visitors saw a valid increase of 116% in donations with a 98% level of confidence. The treatment copy is significantly shorter than the control, so we will need to do additional testing to see if reducing the volume of text and limiting the amount of user scrolling contributed to the lift for mobile visitors. Again, even though we did not see movement in monthly giving as the experiment was intended to produce, we identified learnings to assist in improving conversion rate and overall revenue moving forward for future content offers.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 62.2% increase with a 90% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 33.2% increase in donations with an 88% level of confidence.Female visitors had a 116.0% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 53.3% decrease in donations with a 94% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 116.9% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.New visitors had a 64.4% increase in donations with a 90% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How suggested gift amounts affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Does suggesting gift amounts based upon previous giving increase the revenue and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2015 - 06/22/2015
As part of their fiscal year end campaign, Dallas Theological Seminary wanted to find a way to lower the number of decisions donors had to make when giving their gift. The idea was the suggest a gift amount based upon their previous giving history with the hypothesis that this would lower the friction of the giving form.
To test this hypothesis, we set up an experiment to split traffic between their standard blank gift amount field and a form that would populate three different buttons based upon their last gift amount (this was passed in via the query string).
experiments.keyLearnings:
By suggesting a gift amount, the overall donation page conversion dropped by 54.9%. This was a surprising result given the success of this type of experiment with other research partners.
This outcome taught us something significant about DTS donors. The average gift for their online donors is significantly higher than the most other nonprofits.  As a result, the suggested gift amounts were far larger than would normally be seen on a donation page.  It is our hypothesis that these larger gift options scared away potential donors to the campaign by presuming too much.  We get better results when we let the donor determine their own level of generosity.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing the donation page with experiential language for fiscal year end donors will achieve an increased donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/28/2021 - 08/13/2021
During a fiscal year end campaign, we were seeing lower donor conversion compared to the previous year so we decided to develop a treatment to the donation page for the remainder of the campaign to see if we could improve performance. We modified the headline from “Help DTS reach the world with solid biblical training” to “You can help DTS train students to reach the world with the gospel of Jesus Christ!” We also removed body copy that seemed organization-centric that referenced enrollment trends and their accredited degree programs and instead of featuring the high-caliber quality of the training gave examples of vocations that students pursue after their education and training (counseling, pastoring, etc.) to humanize the institution and presented this as an experiential outcome for how they would “share the good news and strengthen believers” which aligns with what the donor wants to accomplish through their gift. We also introduced copy that highlighted the option to give monthly.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since the experiment only ran during the final 2 days of the campaign, we would need to run an additional test with similar language to truly test the concept without the existing urgency of the deadline that could be influencing results. The treatment donation page had a 19.1% increase in conversion rate with 93% level of confidence. We want to take this directional lift in gifts on the page and see where else we could apply this learning.
While we did not reach statistical significance to validate the experiment, we did observe some notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment that are outlined below:
Revenue experienced a 47.3% decrease with a 97% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
New  visitors had a 39.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 23.9% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 87% level of confidence.55-64 visitors had an 88.5% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Female visitors had a 70.9% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 76.4% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How trustmarks on a donation page impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a Charity Navigator trust mark for donation page visitors will achieve increase in number of gifts and average gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2020 - 12/10/2020
In previous experiments, we have found that adding trust marks near the donation button on a donation page can lead to an increase in donations. However, we wanted to take this learning a step further and test how the presence of ANY trust marks and the presence of CERTAIN trust marks would impact revenue.
The control version of the donation page featured two of the organization’s standard trust marks, with the first treatment removing all trust marks and the second treatment containing the standard trust marks and the additional Charity Navigator trust mark.
The Charity Navigator trust mark showed the organization’s score/ranking of 75% with the “Give with Confidence” banner. The placement for all trust marks was directly beneath the donate button at the bottom of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach a valid level of confidence for the experiment, what we observed through this experiment was the treatment with no trust marks and the treatment with the added Charity Navigator trust mark were both seeing an overall increase in conversion rate. However, when we analyze revenue and average gift performance for each version, the first treatment with no charity marks saw a slight decrease in revenue compared to the control while the second treatment with the additional Charity Navgiator trust mark was approaching a valid lift in revenue (58.3% with a 91% level of confidence). We also noted a few segments that were impacted directly, with an increase in conversion rate for both tablet and email visitors.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 58.3% increase with a 91% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 33.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 55.0% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.
While we will want to further experiment to reach a valid conclusion to identify the optimal version of the form, based on these learnings, we will want to make sure future appeals for tablet and email visitors are pointing to donation pages that indicate evidentials from a credible third party to maximize average gift from these segments and drive overall revenue.

experiments.title: How trustmarks on a donation page impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a Charity Navigator trust mark for donation page visitors will achieve increase in number of gifts and average gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2020 - 12/10/2020
In previous experiments, we have found that adding trust marks near the donation button on a donation page can lead to an increase in donations. However, we wanted to take this learning a step further and test how the presence of ANY trust marks and the presence of CERTAIN trust marks would impact revenue.
The control version of the donation page featured two of the organization’s standard trust marks, with the first treatment removing all trust marks and the second treatment containing the standard trust marks and the additional Charity Navigator trust mark.
The Charity Navigator trust mark showed the organization’s score/ranking of 75% with the “Give with Confidence” banner. The placement for all trust marks was directly beneath the donate button at the bottom of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach a valid level of confidence for the experiment, what we observed through this experiment was the treatment with no trust marks and the treatment with the added Charity Navigator trust mark were both seeing an overall increase in conversion rate. However, when we analyze revenue and average gift performance for each version, the first treatment with no charity marks saw a slight decrease in revenue compared to the control while the second treatment with the additional Charity Navgiator trust mark was approaching a valid lift in revenue (58.3% with a 91% level of confidence). We also noted a few segments that were impacted directly, with an increase in conversion rate for both tablet and email visitors.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 58.3% increase with a 91% level of confidence.
Tablet visitors had a 33.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 55.0% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How the reasons NOT to give affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: By giving reasons NOT to support DTS on Giving Tuesday, will we be able to drive incremental traffic to the donation page and which will lead to an increased number of donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2014 - 12/04/2014
Giving Tuesday has become a powerful fundraising response to the consumerism of Black Friday and Cyber Monday. But with so many nonprofits are soliciting gifts on the same day, it becomes more difficult to stand out in the crowd. DTS tested a contrarian messaging approach that would both intrigue their supporters and increase traffic to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The test treatment was able to increase the click through rate by 528.3% but was not able to convert enough of those visitors to donors to be a statistically valid experiment.  With that said, the results would certainly indicate that this tactic may work in the future as long as we then focus our efforts on optimizing the landing page for the varying motivations of the visitors.

This same tactic of driving an increased number of visitors to the page will be employed in the future but more effort needs to be placed on developing a page that fully addresses the reason for the visit. Our hypothesis is that we did not spend enough time explaining the reasons NOT to give before leading into reasons TO give.

experiments.title: How adjusting the value proposition on a landing page affects instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a revised value proposition on the landing page affect instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/21/2019 - 03/31/2019
DTS was running a value proposition test on their Hebrews landing page, that focused on how the course would benefit the reader rather than focusing on the teacher of the course or the course content itself. We also adjusted the headline of the landing page. The end donation page and following workflow remained the same in both the control and treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test had little to no impact on landing page signups, but something surprising happened in this test. The instant donor conversion rate on the following page from people who signed up on the treatment landing page increased by 35% (not statistically valid, but really close). This could be directly related to the fact that we've just told them the value they're going to get out of this course and the impact it will have on their walk with Christ, rather than focusing our value proposition on course content or course teacher.
Moving forward, further testing is necessary on the value proposition of the landing page and how that flows into the donation page content. Continuing to reiterate the eternal impact of the course is key to higher donor conversion.

experiments.title: How adding testimonials to an online course acquisition page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding 3rd-party evidentials in the form of testimonials increase course conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2018 - 08/03/2018
DTS was running their Genesis online course, and wanted to test the impact of testimonials from other students who had taken the course as 3rd-party evidentials on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The added testimonials did not have a statistically significant impact on course conversion rate. It is our hypothesis that the value expressed by the quotes was cancelled out by the increase of friction that came from a longer landing page. To validate this, we will want to test shorter quotes or have them inline with the email acquisition form.

experiments.title: How suggested gift amounts affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Does suggesting gift amounts based upon previous giving increase the revenue and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2015 - 06/22/2015
As part of their fiscal year end campaign, Dallas Theological Seminary wanted to find a way to lower the number of decisions donors had to make when giving their gift. The idea was the suggest a gift amount based upon their previous giving history with the hypothesis that this would lower the friction of the giving form.
To test this hypothesis, we set up an experiment to split traffic between their standard blank gift amount field and a form that would populate three different buttons based upon their last gift amount (this was passed in via the querystring).
experiments.keyLearnings:
By suggesting a gift amount, the overall donation page conversion dropped by 54.9%. This was a surprising result given the success of this type of experiment with other research partners.
This outcome taught us something significant about DTS donors. The average gift for their online donors is significantly higher than the most other nonprofits.  As a result, the suggested gift amounts were far larger than would normally be seen on a donation page.  It is our hypothesis that these larger gift options scared away potential donors to the campaign by presuming too much.  We get better results when we let the donor determine their own level of generousity.

experiments.title: How the location of a success indicator affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design treatment will produce the highest conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2015 - 07/01/2015
The donation page is the most critical step in online fundraising. Any improvement in the conversion rate on the page, even minor, will lead to increased revenue. During Dallas Theological Seminary’s fiscal year end campaign, we wanted to see if the placement of the campaign progress meter would affect the conversion rate on the form.
Our goal was to gain insight into the donor’s thought sequence but determining where an element that shows process towards a goal should be placed, right at the top of the form or after the impact of a donation has been stated?
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment was able to statistically validate but given the short timeframe and the relatively low sample size, we would want to run this experiment again to be certain of these results.  The increased motivation of donors towards the end of a fiscal year end campaign may have impacted the overall performance of the test.

experiments.title: How the right value proposition can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stronger value proposition increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2015 - 08/05/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary wanted to increase donor conversion rate on their main donation page. Two previous tests had failed to do so by adding value proposition language and changing the open gift field to pre-selected buttons.
The question was raised — what if the first value proposition test had the right language, but the wrong presentation? DTS crafted a new version of the value proposition that started with a fundamental truth that the audience could agree with: “The world needs Biblical truth now more than ever.” This copy also included a quote from Chuck Swindoll that was inline with the text, as opposed to offset in the previous test.
Finally, DTS added a subheadline and strengthened and bolded the call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new landing page produced a 186.5% increase in donor conversion. This told us that presenting the right value proposition in the right way has a massive effect on donor conversion. The results of this test inspired many more tests across different donation pages and acquisition efforts.

experiments.title: How reducing copy for highly motivated donors affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing donation page copy increase conversion for highly motivated donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2015 - 08/07/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary sent a survey to their donors and prospects to better understand what they valued most about the organization. The survey was immediately followed by a donation ask. DTS hypothesized that after completing the survey, donors wouldn’t need as much copy to be convinced to make a gift. So they developed a treatment landing page that removed much of the copy — which to a highly motivated donor, could pose as friction.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page with reduced copy increased conversion by 134.3%. This means that the survey did a sufficient job of motivating the donors — all DTS had to do was ask.

experiments.title: How the wrong value proposition can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will collect the highest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/10/2015 - 06/22/2015
The primary donation form for Dallas Theological Seminary (DTS) had remained consistent for the last several years. It was a well-designed form but it made the assumption that those donors that make it to the page are already convinced to give their gift. This was evident from the lack of any copy or significant calls to action at the top of the form.
In an effort to increase the perceived value of the donation to potential donors, we decided to create a new treatment. This new page would utilize the same donation form but would add copy to the top that discussed one of the unique value propositions of DTS. It also included quotes from well known figures in the Christian community supporting the work of DTS.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new donation form treatment resulted in a 28.1% decrease in donor conversion. This would indicate that the value proposition communicated on the page did not appeal to the potential donors. Additionally, by adding the wrong copy and pushing the donation form further down on the page, we introduced friction into the donation process. These two factors combined to reduce the donors and revenue to the page.

experiments.title: How value proposition affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing perception of cost and work increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2016 - 02/09/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary had a great response to their first free online course on the Gospel of John. But objectively looking at the headline 0f their signup page a few weeks later, they wondered if the value proposition could be improved — specifically, by using something other than the word “study”.
Other experiments had shown that offering the benefits of the offer often outperform words like “study” that make the offer seen like work. So they launched two headline tests to see if they could affect conversion. Both headlines sought to show the real opportunity of the course — to “step inside the classroom”, but with different desired effects. The first headline offered the opportunity to “see the gospel of John like never before” and the other to “grow your understanding of John’s gospel”.
They launched an A/B/C test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Surprisingly, neither variation outperformed the control. This tells us something very interesting — “study” might actually be a positive factor of the value proposition. It suggests that prospects for this course might be looking to “study” and really spend time with the course.
It may also suggest that the value proposition to “step inside the classroom” is unclear or undesirable. This concept lends itself to further testing.

experiments.title: How registration form length affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing the perceived length of the form increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2016 - 06/27/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary had just released their third free online course on Genesis. Although they had made plenty of changes to the landing page copy, they hadn’t tried to optimize the form, which asked for both online and offline information, totaling 11 fields. We hypothesized that reducing the perceived length of the form (by nesting fields together) would increase conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings: The optimized form produced a 4.1% increase in signups. This slight increase suggested that the form didn’t have as much impact on the page as originally thought, and prompted a series of new tests to increase the force of the value proposition in the copy.

experiments.title: How additional premium-focused value proposition affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the addition of premium focused value proposition copy increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2016 - 09/19/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary had found a popular offer in their free Gospel of John course. After the user signed up, they were given the opportunity to make a gift.In exchange for a gift of $100 or more they were offered a commentary as the course companion book for the Gospel of John course. DTS decided to try to see if they could increase conversion rate by integrating review content for the commentary into the donation page. They pulled some reviews from a popular book review site onto the page, showed five stars, and gave a positive review for the commentary, hoping that this would increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page produced a 47% decrease in conversion, although these results were not statistically valid due to low traffic to the page. However, the treatment also produced an increase in average gift, which may suggested this motivated a certain segment of people who enrolled in the course to increase their average gift to receive the commentary. However, this additional value proposition appeared to distract from the main value proposition, which converted more registered users into donors. Wally learning to not produce a positive impact on this donation page further testing for how the book is offered throughout the course might produce a lift.

experiments.title: How adding a soft donation ask to an email about a survey impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a soft ask for contacts that received the FYE survey email will achieve more donations.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/08/2025
DTS was sending their annual FYE Survey before launching their FYE campaign. We decided to test how adding a line in the email that said “After completing the survey, you’ll also have the opportunity to deepen your partnership with DTS if you choose.” would impact donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a soft ask for donations in the email sent to contacts receiving the FYE survey resulted in a significant increase in donations. The treatment group, which included the line about deepening the partnership with DTS, saw a 95.3% increase in donations compared to the control group.

However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 73.8%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing the impact of including a donation ask in communications with contacts. By conducting further tests with larger sample sizes and ensuring a higher level of statistical significance, we can determine more accurately if this strategy is effective in driving donations for Dallas Theological Seminary.

experiments.title: How using donor autonomous language on an instant donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Using donor autonomous language on an instant donation page after an individual signs up for a free online course will increase the donation amount because people won’t feel marketed to, but realize they have the power to make the decision.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2024 - 10/28/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary wants to reach more people worldwide with biblical-truth and teach people how to study the Bible. To this aim, they offer free online courses. All their courses are funded by generous donors. After a person signs up for the Revelation course they are re-directed to an instant donation page. This page thanks them for signing up and then asks then to donate.
In an attempt to increase the donor amount on this page, they wanted to test whether donor autonomous language would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using donor autonomous language on an instant donation page after an individual signs up for a free online course can potentially increase the donation amount. While the treatment did see a 6.3% increase in Revenue for All Traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 64.5%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the statistical significance of the results before drawing conclusions. It may be beneficial to repeat the experiment with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the donation page to further test the impact of donor autonomous language. Additionally, it could be useful to explore other factors that may influence donation amounts, such as the design of the donation page or the timing of the donation request. By continuing to test and analyze different variables, Dallas Theological Seminary can gather more reliable data to inform their decision-making process and optimize their donation strategy.

experiments.title: How the placement of the call to action impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adjusting the CTA placement for landing page viewers will achieve more email acquisitions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2024 - 09/10/2024
DTS runs courses for email acquisition. For these courses, they use a course platform that has a template for what the landing page looks like. The template has the headline underneath the CTA. The user also has to scroll to see it. We wanted to test putting the headline above the CTA to see how it impacted email acquisitions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adjusting the CTA placement for landing page viewers resulted in a 11.2% decrease in Email Acquisitions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 42.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to yield statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to run the experiment for a longer period of time to account for any fluctuations in traffic or other external factors that could impact the results. Furthermore, it would be beneficial to consider other factors that could potentially influence email acquisitions, such as the design of the CTA button itself or the messaging around it.

experiments.title: How a callout on a gift array impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a callout on the gift array for donation page visitors will achieve more donations because it increases the value proposition. because it increases the value proposition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2024 - 06/30/2024
DTS was running their annual FYE campaign. We wanted to test how a callout that added value proposition would impact donations and encourage people to give a gift of a higher amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a callout on the gift array for donation page visitors can potentially increase donations, as evidenced by a 10.4% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic in the treatment group compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 50.2%, which is below the threshold of 95% needed for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test different types of callouts or messaging to see which ones resonate best with donors and lead to the most significant increase in donations. It is also important to continuously iterate and experiment with different strategies to optimize donation performance.

experiments.title: How bypassing the registration page impacted email signups and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing friction in the course registration process for landing page viewers will achieve an increase in email acquisition because there will be less friction in the process and fewer clicks to achieve the desired action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/20/2023 - 12/18/2023
After moving to a new course platform, we wanted to test reducing friction in the process by not having visitors have to click an additional button to begin the registration process. However, that page that preceded the checkout option is what communicated the value proposition of the course to the reader so we wanted to test before changing the process entirely. We set up an A/B test and split traffic to the registration page URL and the other 50% of traffic went to a treatment page which bypassed the registration page and went straight to the course checkout page. We ran the experiment for 2 months and observed how visitors behaved on the email acquisition page as well as downstream impacts to guide decisions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the registration page and reducing friction in the course registration process resulted in a decrease in email acquisition. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

To determine the impact of reducing friction on the registration process and email acquisition, it is recommended to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and longer durations. This will help obtain more statistically significant results and provide more reliable insights.

Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore other factors that might have influenced the decrease in email acquisition. For example, the treatment page may have lacked important information or a clear value proposition, leading to lower engagement and conversions.

Future experiments can also focus on improving the treatment page by incorporating elements of the registration page that effectively communicate the value proposition of the course. This way, the experiment can gauge whether reducing friction while maintaining the value proposition can positively impact email acquisition. It is important to analyze and understand user behavior and preferences to optimize the registration process and maximize email acquisition.

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer on a recurring campaign donation pages impacts monthly donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer for DTS’s North Texas Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion because the thermometer adds clarity on the number of people still needed to become a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2023 - 09/21/2023
Each year, Dallas Theological Seminary (DTS) participates in the local giving day, North Texas Giving Day. During previous years, they’ve focused on increasing their number of monthly donors—Cornerstone Partners.
This year, they continued in this tradition, but wanted to see what optimizations they could make to their donation page to see if they could increase recurring donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment version of the donation page, which included a monthly donor counter thermometer, resulted in a 23.2% increase in recurring donations compared to the control version.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 64.4%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid.

3. The hypothesis that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer would increase monthly donor conversions was supported by the observed increase in recurring donations.

4. Future experiments should consider further optimizing the donation page by including a monthly donor counter thermometer as it showed promising results in increasing recurring donor conversions.

5. It is important to have a larger sample size and a higher level of statistical confidence to validate the findings and make informed decisions for future experimentation.

experiments.title: How enhancing the personal value for the user of a free online course impacted email registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the value to the user for potential course takers will achieve an increase in email acquisition because the course is then positioned more uniquely and personally as a solution to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2023 - 10/18/2023
When reviewing a course with low sign up rate, we wanted to propose a landing page treatment that would enhance the value of the course to the individual visitor. We put together copy that focuses more on the historical debates and questions about the return of Christ—addressing the questions in the minds of the visitors that would be answered or solved by signing up for the free online course. We attempt to tap into the curiosity of the visitor, positioning the course as a solution to those uncertainties. We also added clarity in the call to action to make it clear what next step the visitor should take before introducing the button to register. The treatment specifically mentions that by signing up, users will receive their first lecture instantly. This adds a sense of immediacy and instant gratification which might entice visitors to take action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing the value of the course to potential users can result in an increase in email acquisition. This highlights the importance of positioning the course as a unique and personal solution to the uncertainties and questions that potential users may have.

In future experimentation, we should continue to focus on highlighting the value and benefits of the courses we offer. This could involve addressing specific pain points or uncertainties that potential users may have and demonstrating how our courses can provide the solutions or answers they are seeking.

Additionally, it is important to remember the significance of clarity in the call to action. Clearly stating the next steps for the visitor and highlighting any immediate benefits, such as receiving the first lecture instantly, can help to create a sense of urgency and incentivize users to take action.

To validate the findings of this experiment and make informed decisions for future experimentation, it is necessary to run additional experiments with a larger sample size and higher statistical level of confidence. This will provide more reliable and valid results that can be used to guide future decision-making.

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer on a recurring campaign donation pages impacts monthly donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer for DTS’s North Texas Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion because the thermometer adds clarity on the number of people still needed to become a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2023 - 09/21/2023
Each year, Dallas Theological Seminary (DTS) participates in the local giving day, North Texas Giving Day. During previous years, they’ve focused on increasing their number of monthly donors—Cornerstone Partners.
This year, they continued in this tradition, but wanted to see what optimizations they could make to their donation page to see if they could increase recurring donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment version of the donation page, which included a monthly donor counter thermometer, resulted in a 23.2% increase in recurring donations compared to the control version.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 64.4%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid.

3. The hypothesis that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer would increase monthly donor conversions was supported by the observed increase in recurring donations.

4. Future experiments should consider further optimizing the donation page by including a monthly donor counter thermometer as it showed promising results in increasing recurring donor conversions.

5. It is important to have a larger sample size and a higher level of statistical confidence to validate the findings and make informed decisions for future experimentation.

experiments.title: How reducing organizational references and using donor-centric language impacted gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using donor-centric language for course subscribers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there is stronger appeal to give based on value and belief alignment with the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/18/2024 - 07/19/2024
When evaluating a donation page that appeared for course subscribers, we wondered if the language was too centered around the organization and not focused on the page viewer. We hypothesized we could convince more potential donors to say “yes” to the ask if we treated the donation page like a conversation and used the messaging to align with the page visitors’ beliefs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using donor-centric language for course subscribers can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion. The treatment group in the A/B experiment saw a 12.8% increase in one-time donations for all traffic, at 43% level of confidence. Results of past tests of this concept suggest that there is a stronger appeal to give when the messaging aligns with the page visitors' beliefs and values, but this test did not validate.

In future experimentation, it would be important to continue exploring the use of donor-centric language and messaging that focuses on the values and beliefs of the potential donors. Additionally, incorporating feedback from donors and continuously iterating on the messaging based on donor responses could also help in optimizing donation pages for increased conversion rates.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition of a sticky bar impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the value proposition for the course sticky bar will achieve an increase in email conversions because it increases the clarity of what someone will receive when they sign up for the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2024 - 02/13/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary regularly runs sticky bars on their voice.dts.edu website to advertise new courses, surveys, and more. They’ve previously tested into language that acknowledges them as a friend of DTS and use this language regularly.
For their new course, we wanted to test different language angles to see if we could get more people clicking into the course landing page.
For the first treatment, we wanted to test language about starting off the new year with a new course as it launched in January.
For the second treatment, we focused on the felt need that the course addresses.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results showed that the treatment version resulted in a 3.1% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 65.7%, falling below the standard threshold of 95% for validity. As a result, while the treatment won, the results cannot be considered reliable for future decision-making.

The key learning from this experiment is that changing the value proposition in the sticky bar can impact email conversions. However, to draw more concrete conclusions, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be necessary. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure a sufficient level of statistical confidence to make reliable decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: How adding clarity around the nature of an offer using an image impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity with a digital image for housefile email recipients will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because there will be enhanced clarity around the nature of the offer they are signing up for.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/25/2023 - 12/09/2023
When sending a Christmas devotional offer, DTS wanted to add clarity to potential readers that this was a digital offer and not a physical book since some of their other premiums are a physical copy of a particular resource. We set up a test to determine if additional clarity in the email would impact click rates from the housefile.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we saw after running the experiment was the treatment that added a digital image for housefile email recipients resulted in a measured increase of only 0.1%, and it only reached a 1.3% level of confidence so the experiment results are not valid and the results are flat.

This will have to be tested on future content offers to get a better understanding of how housefile email recipients are motivated.

experiments.title: How removing a signer’s image in an email footer signature impacted clicks

rows.name: Control - With Image in Footer Signature
Treatment - No Image in Footer Signature

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a photo from the email signature for housefile recipients will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because the email will appear more human and personalized.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2023 - 12/14/2023
When sending a course registration email, we wondered if the signature image was causing the email template to appear more like marketing and less like a personalized email coming from a real human. We split the file 50/50 and tested each version to measure engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
