The key learning from this experiment is that removing a photo from the email signature for housefile recipients resulted in a 7.5% increase in clickthrough rate. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.8%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.
We will want to re-test this concept in future experiments to fully measure the impact of removing the photo from the email signature.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array in the body of an email appeal impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a sample gift array for a direct email appeal will achieve an increase in engagement because there will be additional cognitive momentum to continue the process to the next step.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 11/28/2023
In a high-urgency campaign, we typically keep the decisions isolated per each step. In the email step, we help the reader decide “Do I want to click?” and then at the donation page step we help the reader decide “How much do I want to give?” In one email, we wanted to test if adding gift array options at the email level would help more people begin thinking about both questions at once. The treatment version added a gift array of buttons, letting the reader know they could click one of the buttons to choose their donation amount and begin the process of making a monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: After splitting the email between the control and the treatment, we observed a 1.2% lift in clicks at 9.4% level of confidence for the treatment version which is not statistically significant so the experiment results are not valid. The engagement remained flat, even when we added buttons with gift array amounts in the body of the email. We will want to look downstream and see if there is any significant difference in completed donations from the treatment group that would lead to more meaningful results to see if moving the gift array into the body copy helped produce more motivated visits from email traffic who had already decided what amount they would be interested in giving and continue the cognitive momentum in the process when they arrive on the donation page.

experiments.title: How the presentation of a digital resource on a landing page impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting the resource in a digital form for landing page viewers will achieve an increased email sign up rate because they have clarity in the way they will be able to use the digital resource. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2023 - 09/21/2023
Dallas Theological Seminary has participated in North Texas Giving Day each year. From previous testing, they know that priming their housefile with a content offer has historically lead to increased donors and revenue. This year, they wanted to test if presenting the eBook in a different method on the landing page would impact email sign ups.
Historically with eBooks, they have created an image that represents the eBook as a 3D eBook preview—more like an actual book. They decided to test this against a mock-up of the eBook on an iPad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting the resource in a digital form on the landing page resulted in a slight increase in email signups compared to the traditional 3D eBook preview. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is only 40.1%, which is below the acceptable threshold of 85%.
Based on these results, it is not advisable to make any definitive conclusions or decisions regarding the effectiveness of presenting the eBook in a digital form. The results of this experiment should not be used for future decision-making.
In order to obtain more reliable and valid results, it is recommended to conduct further testing with a larger sample size and a higher statistical level of confidence. By doing so, the nonprofit can gather more accurate and conclusive data to determine the most effective method of presenting the eBook on the landing page.
DTS will be testing this again during Calendar Year End on their Christmas Devotional, which has historically gotten a significantly higher rate of traffic and should be able to give us a better sample size.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline prompting readers to read on impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a continuation headline  for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because page visitors will learn that there is more to their journey and will realize their opportunity to make a gift and why a gift is needed.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2023 - 07/02/2023
DTS’ James course offer is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. When user’s sign up for the James online course, they are redirected to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to give. We want to see if we can get more people to give after signing up for the course.
We’ve built a donation page headline test that applies a continuation headline. The control donation page headline says “Thank you” and “Congratulations”, implying that their journey has ended and that nothing else if needed of them.
We want to change this implication to rather encourage users to continue on. That’s why we’ve built a treatment headline that reads as follows:
Before you go…
Your course material for week 1 is headed to your inbox – we’re excited for you to get started! But before you go, would you consider one more thing?
We believe this headline will encourage readers to read on, enabling them to engage with the value proposition copy and to see that donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings: After being able to run for only 8 days, the treatment saw a 2.9% decrease in donations with only a 4.3% level of confidence. It led to a more notable 40.9% decrease in revenue (77.1% level of confidence). As we were constrained by the sunset of the testing platform that this experiment was being run on, we will have to test this experiment once more on a new platform to give it more runway and to determine more trustworthy data outcomes.

experiments.title: How donor-centric language impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that donor-centric value proposition for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the value proposition and reasons to give will pertain to them on their impact rather than the organization’s.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2023 - 07/02/2023
DTS’ Genesis course offer is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. When user’s sign up for the Genesis online course, they are redirected to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to give. We want to see if we can get more people to give after signing up for the course.
We’ve developed a treatment which focuses especially on the donor. It includes much “you” language and focuses on the donor’s perspective and what they can help accomplish.
This differs from the control page, which uses much organization-centric language – “we” language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 8 days, the treatment produced a 45.1% increase in donor conversion rate with a 43% level of confidence. It also produced a 30.6% lift in revenue with a 65% level of confidence. Unfortunately, external factors prevented this experiment from running any longer.
Despite the limitations, this promising trend may prove this to be a experiment worth running for your organization.
The treatment was trending in the right direction, but before we can roll it out, we'll need to test it for a longer period of time.

experiments.title: How reinforcing security upon course sign up affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing security for course takers will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate because there is reduced anxiety about how their information will be used .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2023 - 07/02/2023
When signing up for a course there may be added anxiety as to why we may be asking for their contact information. Adding security language below the submit button can let them know that their information is safe with DTS. Our hypothesis is that the addition of this language will encourage more people to fill out the form at the end and lead more people to the instant donation page (also resulting in an increase in donor conversion rate).
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 22 days, the treatment (security language implemented) produced a 1.6% increase in email acquisition rate with a 40% level of confidence. Given the flat lift and modest level of confidence, we cannot confidently roll out the treatment. It seems the added security language had little impact on a user's motivation and comfort to provide their personal information and sign up for the course. We may want to try a similar test on the instant donation page to see if it provides a more significant impact. This could be a more effective place to run the test given the additional information required from someone in order to complete a donation.

experiments.title: How urgency-focused value proposition copy impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that urgency value proposition language for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because visitors will feel a sense of urgency to give and make an impact that they otherwise wouldn’t.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2023 - 06/23/2023
DTS is amid a high-urgency fiscal year-end campaign. As our org is pushing a large number of users to our fiscal year-end donation pages, we want to test into urgency-focused donation page copy.
As part of our FYE campaign, we launched a free content offer where users can sign up to receive the eBook, and once they do, they’re redirected an instant donation page. Our existing donation page deploys standard value proposition messaging through problem/solution copy and reasons to give; however, it does not include important FYE messaging including urgency language, the campaign deadline, campaign goal, or matching gift challenge associated with the campaign. The only urgent element it includes is a countdown clock, but it does not explain what the clock is counting down to.
We want to test urgency-focused messaging that does include these elements and mentions, and that grabs the user’s attention immediately, alerting them of this urgent opportunity to make an impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The urgency-focused language did not produce the results that we expected. After running for 17 days, the treatment resulted in a 9.6% decrease in donations with a 40.4% level of confidence. It also produced a 4% decrease in revenue with a 56.7% level of confidence.
The key learning from this experiment is that urgency-focused language does not always lead to more donations. It can provide the urgency, the flash, and the untraditional that could inspire someone to give. Though, in this case, I wonder if the supporting value proposition copy was not as strong as the control. The control includes strong problem/solution copy and highlights specifically how a donor's gift will be used, while the treatment includes more general value proposition copy that never really explains the problem(s) that a donor's gift might help solve and how the gift would be used to solve them.
For a future iteration, it would be wise to blend the value proposition copy from the control with the urgency elements used in the treatment. This could be the formula for increase donor conversion and revenue.

experiments.title: How adding appeal and exclusivity to course sign up value proposition copy impacted sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe enhancing elements of appeal and exclusivity for landing page visitors will increase sign up rate because visitors will gain an improved understanding of what they’ll get out of the course and how the course will improve their relationship with Christ.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/29/2023
In an effort to increase sign up rate to DTS’ Intro to Prophets online course, we built a sign up page treatment focusing on enhancing the appeal and exclusivity with the value proposition copy. We feel these elements are lacking in the existing copy.
For exclusivity, we added language including “Don’t miss this rare opportunity”, “This one-of-a-kind course”, and “become part of an exclusive community of like-minded learners.”
For appeal, we transitioned the copy to focus more on the potential course participant and the course itself instead of the organization and the organization’s mission, and added more action sentences to hopefully invoke the action of signing up.
Let’s see how the adjusted value prop performs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that enhancing elements of appeal and exclusivity can potentially increase the sign-up rate for a course. However, the statistical level of confidence of 32.6% is well below the threshold of 85% and the results cannot be considered valid.
The treatment produced a minimal 0.98% lift in course sign ups.
Downstream metrics were minimal as well, with the treatment producing a 5% increase in donations with just a 22.5% level of confidence, and revenue decreasing by 2.7% with a 55.7% level of confidence.
The added appeal and exclusivity did not enhance user motivation and increase course sign up like we hypothesized it would. We will need to revisit the value proposition copy to brainstorm alternative motives to increase sign ups. Or consider testing opportunities elsewhere on the sign up page.
Further experiments need to be conducted with a larger sample size and more significant changes to the treatment. Future experiments should focus on creating a strong value proposition that directly addresses the needs of the potential course participants and highlights the unique features of the course. Additionally, the experiment should explore other factors that could potentially impact the sign-up rate, such as the design of the landing page and the placement of the call-to-action button. Overall, the experiment provides a starting point for further exploration of how to increase sign-up rates for online courses, but a larger sample size and more significant changes are needed to draw valid conclusions.

experiments.title: How including student testimonials on a course landing page impacted signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including student testimonials on the course sign up landing page will increase course sign ups because it establishes more credibility for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2023 - 04/01/2023
In an effort to increase the number of course sign ups on the our Genesis course sign up landing page, we wanted to know how including testimonials of previous students on the course sign up page would impact the number of individuals who enrolled in our Genesis course.
For this experiment, we ran an A/B test splitting traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment variants. The control version modeled the standard landing page format for all courses, while the treatment version included a text box of two different quotes regarding this course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment aimed to determine the impact of including student testimonials on the Genesis course signup landing page to increase course signups. Results showed that the treatment resulted in a 4.9% decrease in course signups with a statistical level of confidence of 63.7%. Although the control variant won, the results cannot be considered valid since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%.
Key learnings from this experiment include the importance of setting a higher statistical level of confidence for more valid results, considering the impact on specific audience segments, and the need for further experimentation on the use of testimonials on course signup landing pages. Future experimentation could focus on testing different variations of testimonials, targeting specific audience segments, and testing other elements on the landing page that could impact course signups.
We chose to end this experiment before the 30 day mark due to results consistently being in the negatives.
The key learning from this experiment is that including student testimonials on the course sign up landing page may not always be effective in increasing course sign ups, and further testing will be necessary.

experiments.title: How an alternate motive conveyed in the course landing page headline impacted course sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing the headline on personal application and self-improvement for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because visitors will be more interested in how the course can improve their life in faith rather than deepening their knowledge of a book of the bible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2023 - 03/26/2023
The Dallas Theological Seminary’s James course landing page is producing a 19.1% sign-up rate and $7.57 gross cost per name – both metrics are missing their benchmark. In an attempt to improve these results, we’ve developed a landing page test that tests what the user hopes to get out of the course.
Looking at the current headline, we wonder if it effectively speaks to the user’s desire. Do they want to deepen their knowledge of a book of the bible or do they want to take the overall message of the course and apply it to their life in faith? Are they looking for knowledge or self-improvement?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment version that emphasized action and how the course could improve the visitor's life in faith resulted in a 3.5% increase in course signups, albeit with a statistical level of confidence of only 61.9%. This means that the findings cannot be deemed significant or conclusive for future decision-making.
Moving further down the funnel, the treatment lifted donation conversion by 6.2% but with a 16.6% level of confidence. And revenue experienced a 16% decrease through the treatment with a 70% level of confidence. None of the metrics validated through this test.
The key learning from this experiment is that a headline that emphasizes personal application and self-improvement could increase course signup rates, but the data overall is not consistent or significant enough to determine an optimal approach through this headline test. The collective audience in the test did not lean favorably in either direction when it comes to desirability or motivation to sign up for the course. The next test we run on this page should have more significant changes to trigger more conversions and a more significant level of confidence.

experiments.title: How encouraging a user to read on in the headline of an instant donation page affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including language that encourages users to read more on an instant donation page following an online course registration will increase instant conversion rates by eliminating the cue that there is nothing else to do.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2022 - 12/23/2022
In an effort to increase donation rates on an instant donation page that is shown to users after they complete their registration, we wanted to know how including language in the headline that encourages users to keep reading would help increase donation rates.
For this experiment, we ran an A/B test, splitting traffic 50/50 between the control (featuring a headline that contained only the word “Congratulations”) and the treatment (which included confirmation language and the phrase, “Before you start…”) and ran the experiment for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, this experiment yielded flat results and did not receive a large enough sample size to validate the results (a 2.2% decrease in donations with only a 9.94% level of confidence), preventing us from drawing definitive conclusions on the efficacy of our hypothesis based on this experiment.
We may look to run this experiment for another online course in an attempt to validate results ... or we may look to test a more significant change to the headline such as presenting the value of making a donation front and center rather than one based so heavily on using confirmation language, i.e., giving the user a compelling reason to read on rather than using a phrase like "before you go"

experiments.title: How Calling Out The Name of a Monthly Giving Program on Tabbed Donation Form Affects Monthly Giving Rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing generic monthly donation form tab copy with program-specific copy will increase recurring donor rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/13/2022 - 10/02/2022
In an effort to combat ‘messaging fatigue,’ we wanted to know how replacing the generic monthly donation tab copy with language that specifically calls out the name of their recurring giving program will affect monthly giving. We replaced “Recurring monthly gift” with “Become a Cornerstone Partner” to see if we could increase exclusivity and a feeling of community by calling out the program by name. We ran this experiment during DTS’s annual North Texas Giving Day campaign, and split traffic 50/50
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 16 days, we saw a 155% increase in monthly donation rates with a 75% level of confidence. Although there was a low volume of monthly gifts (restricted by the shorter length of a campaign), we were encouraged to see a significant lift in recurring giving via the treatment which included the "Become a Cornerstone Partner"--the name of Dallas Theological Seminary's monthly giving program.
We believe that calling the monthly giving program out by name was able to add value in the eyes of donors by conveying exclusivity and a sense of community/belonging. We also believe that breaking away from more generic 'give monthly' language helped the monthly giving option stand out by reducing "messaging fatigue" and thus doing a better job of catching the donor's attention.
Based on these results, we will look to validate this hypothesis by rolling the same treatment out to higher-traffic donation pages that are not as restricted by the truncated timeframe of a high-urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation page impacted gifts during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radically redesigned donation page for fiscal year end campaign visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2022 - 08/01/2022
DTS was running their annual fiscal year-end campaign and we wanted to optimize the standard donation page that is typically used during this campaign. We developed a treatment that incorporated the following elements: we updated the headline to include urgency by referencing the deadline of “before midnight on June 30”. We also used bullet points to demonstrate opportunities for impact in the body copy. The language also gave specific evidentials of how the donor’s gift would be used, stating that for every $75 you give, you can offer our online courses to 15 people. We also introduced some donor autonomy and re-highlighted the urgency of the deadline in the subheader leading into the donation form “If you want to support…please make your gift using the form below:”
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the test for the duration of the campaign was that the radical redesign treatment had a 5% decrease in conversion rate with 44% level of confidence so the results are not valid. Our hypothesis for the flat results is that 1) the donation page copy is longer than the control which could have introduced additional friction to the experience for those in the treatment group and 2) the donation page copy used in the treatment is still organization-centric and not donor-focused. All 4-5 paragraphs begin with the word "DTS" or "we" and the bullet points are organization-centric in terms of being presented as "here's what we are already doing" rather than posed as "what your gift will help accomplish". We also feel that the urgency could have been better communicated by asking for a gift "now" or "today" rather than any time before June 30. We need to better compel visitors to make their gift at the time of their visit to the page. There is no clear need or problem presented in the copy in the treatment for the donor to address or solve, which is something we will want to consider framing out for future tests.

experiments.title: How making an ask for a specific program impacted donation page results – Part III
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a program-specific value proposition for free online course takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2022 - 03/06/2022
As we looked to increase donor conversion for free online courses, we wanted to find what messaging would most resonate with donors. The existing donation page standard copy was centered on the value proposition of getting the free course into the hands of more people. We wanted to test if transitioning the value proposition to a more specific outcome would motivate donation page visitors. We developed the first treatment to focus on using their gift to promote free online courses to pastors to supplement their training and meet this specific need/problem that exists. We developed a second treatment that highlighted current efforts to translate the free online courses into additional languages to expand their reach, which will require additional funding to build and promote. We wanted to find out if either of these treatments would increase donor conversion by addressing the needs at hand the organization is trying to meet and communicating a specific impact that the donor’s gift would be supporting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the split test across all 3 versions for one month on the donation page. What we found was the first treatment that emphasized pastoral training was mostly flat and saw a 6% increase with 19% level of confidence. On the second treatment however, we learned that the translation of courses seemed to be lifting donor conversion and at the end of the experiment resulted in a 16% increase with 48% level of confidence. Because we saw similar results on two additional online course donation pages that were running concurrently, we made the decision to roll out the second treatment as the new control for this donation page experience. We will continue to find opportunities to optimize this copy in future testing.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 67.5% increase in donations with an 90% level of confidence.Social visitors had a 7.9% increase in donations with an 85% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had an 11.1% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.35-44 visitors had a 10.3% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.18-24 visitors had a 55.8% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 10.3% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a recurring gift pop-up impacted recurring gifts on Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup with a recurring ask for one-time donors will achieve an increase in monthly donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2021 - 01/07/2022
For previous giving day campaigns, we had seen an increase in overall donor lifetime value and retention for monthly donors. We wanted to find a way to convert one time donors to monthly donors on Giving Tuesday and decided to test a recurring giving pop-up at the point of transaction for one time gifts. The popup intercepted the user before their gift was processed and urged them to convert their one-time gift to monthly instead and reiterated the benefits of giving a monthly gift. We displayed the pop-up to 50% of traffic during the giving day campaign to evaluate its effectiveness.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Because the campaign had a very limited window to run, we did not receive enough sessions or transactions to validate the experiment. We will want to test this again with a longer runway to capture a proper amount of traffic in order to more thoroughly analyze how this impacts recurring giving in the future.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 215.6% increase in recurring donation with an 90% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How the value proposition copy on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the value prop copy treatment that we validated on the Genesis course stand on its own in the Revelation course, or does it only work when coupled with the removal of the premium offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2019 - 04/06/2019
DTS validated a copy test on the Genesis donation page that increased donations by 248% (experiment id: 10582), but also included the removal of a high-dollar premium offer from the page. They wanted to test the separation of the copy from the premium removal, so they tested the same copy on the Revelation course donation page, while still including the premium offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not reach statistical validity and only impacted conversion rate slightly. We are going to re-run this test with the removal of the premium to see if the same concept that worked on the Genesis course works here.

experiments.title: How personalization on a donation page impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalization on an instant donation page after a content offer increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2020 - 10/02/2020
As part of a giving day campaign, DTS was offering a content offer and wanted to test using the donor’s first name in the headline to see if this would drive conversion rate on the instant donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach validity with this experiment, when evaluating the results and drilling down even further by each segment, we noticed that while overall conversion rate decreased on the page, we saw an 89.4% lift in average gift—specifically with the returning visitors segment—with an 88% level of confidence. Across all traffic we saw a 40.8% lift in average gift with an 82% level of confidence. These findings in the directional lift in average gift will be used for future testing and will be useful for developing what language we present on donation pages to returning visitors moving forward.

experiments.title: How adding a recurring donor ticker affected a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including the live total number of donors acquired towards the overall goal increase conversion rate on a donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/15/2020 - 09/24/2020
With a specific goal set for a high urgency campaign, we wanted to test if adding a ticker to the donation page in a sticky bar to count the number of monthly donors would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach the minimum level of confidence needed for validity of the experiment and saw a slight decrease in the number of transactions, we did notice that the average gift increased on the treatment, which is something we would want to test further to evaluate. We also hypothesized that the results could have been negatively influenced since the campaign met its monthly donor goal ahead of schedule, there may have been a reduced sense of need or urgency when visitors arrived on the page and saw the goal had already been met before they decided to contribute. For future tests, we want to launch the sticky bar earlier in the campaign to see how it affects motivation and we also want to test replacing the text-only version sticky bar with additional visuals like a progress bar or thermometer to further add clarity to the progress of the goal and what is being counted.

experiments.title: How removing a high-dollar premium from a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does removing a high-dollar premium from a donation page increase instant donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2019 - 05/03/2019
DTS was running a test on their Revelation donation page that utilized copy that had just increased donations on the Genesis course in a previous experiment, but also removed a high-dollar premium that they hypothesized was leading to fewer instant donations, because they thought it was possible that people didn’t want to give at the level necessary to get the book ($75).
experiments.keyLearnings: This test didn't validate, but showed a slight trend towards a lower instant conversion rate with the treatment. These results are likely to be better analyzed in future testing if the same copy is used on both control and treatment, but that the premium offer's placement on the page is an isolated variable.

experiments.title: How the framing of the transition on the instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does framing the transition into the donation ask differently in the headline + body copy affect donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2018 - 08/03/2018
For Dallas Theological Seminary, we wanted to see if we can reframe the donation page that follows the Genesis online course into a broader value proposition. This copy approach leads in more quickly into the donation ask and value proposition than the control, which focuses more on the next steps for the actual course, rather than the big picture value of these online courses to believers everywhere.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results were not statistically valid, but we did see a trend towards a 25% lift in donations with the treatment. Further testing necessary to validate this learning.

experiments.title: How simplifying the email design affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: A simplified email will have a more personal feel to it which will prompt a greater number of click thoughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2014 - 12/22/2014
The standard format for DTS emails is a designed layout with images and a color scheme that matches the website. In an effort to increase the number of visitors to the calendar year end campaign, we created an experiment to test the standard design against a simplified email.
experiments.keyLearnings: The design of the email did not have any significant impact on the supporter’s likelihood of clicking on the email. It also did not have any significant impact on the donors’ propensity to give. Moving forward we will utilize the existing email templates to save both cost and time.

experiments.title: Understanding the perceived value of video to email recipients
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using text instead of a video will allow donors to focus on the call-to-action which will increase the response rate of the campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2014 - 12/30/2014
Video is often used as a way to increase visitor engagement on donation landing pages. However, it was our hypothesis that the videos actual distracted from the ultimate goal, giving. As a result, we created an experiment to test a video against a text version of the same message.
experiments.keyLearnings: With no significant difference between click rates and nearly identical response rates (both are 0.1%), the effort put into the video may not be necessary this late in a year end campaign.

experiments.title: How value proposition conveyed through headlines affects signups
experiments.researchQuestion: Headline’s emphasizing what will be gained by giving their email address will have a higher conversion rate than framing it as a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2014 - 12/21/2015
During the Christmas season, DTS creates a devotional series that runs between December 1st until Christmas. As a way to broaden the audience that receives the devotionals and collect new email addresses, we created a landing page to be used for Facebook advertising. We then created a new treatment to find the message the resonated most with the Facebook audience
experiments.keyLearnings: Given the minor difference in acquisition rate, it would appear the two treatments were not distinct enough to elicit an increase in response.

experiments.title: How the headline value proposition affects acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: By stating what the visitor will “get” within the headline, we can improve the conversion rate on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2014 - 12/14/2014
As part of the calendar year end campaign, DTS produced an eBook that they gave away free to their supporters. To expand the impact of this free offer, we also created landing pages that were advertised to Facebook audiences. These pages offered the book for the price of an email address.
With the influx of traffic, we used the opportunity to test the impact that a headline can have on conveying the value proposition of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The audience was already highly motivated and the ads had conveyed what would be received on the subsequent page. As a result, the headlines did not produce a significant lift to conversion rate.

experiments.title: How the cost of a premium affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a premium boost the overall conversion and revenue of a donation landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2015 - 06/24/2015
As part of the a name acquisition campaign, Dallas Theological Seminary was giving away a free eBook in exchange for a visitor’s email address.  After the successful email acquisition, visitors were sent to a contextualized donation page.  In an effort to boost conversion on this follow up donation ask, we wanted to see if offering a relevant premium (a hardcover book on a similar topic) would boost conversion.
As a result, we created two landing pages, with one that had a premium offer for a gift of any amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The donation page with the premium offer did not have any statistically significant impact on either conversion rate or revenue. Since this book had an associated cost to the seminary, it didn’t make sense to continue offering the premium to donors.
For many organizations, premium offers will often have a positive impact on donor conversion and average gift but there are times where, as in this case, the impact was negligible. This is why it is so critical to test any changes to a donation form to ensure the impact with worth the cost.

experiments.title: How value proposition affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition delivers a higher donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2015 - 10/26/2015
DTS launched a resource center for biblical perspectives on today’s issues. The goal of this resource center was name and donor acquisition. Immediately after the signup page was a donation ask. DTS wanted to test messaging on the donation page to determine which value proposition was stronger: an ask for an investment in a student or an ask centered around teaching biblical truth.
experiments.keyLearnings: DTS ran this test for several months, with no clear winner. We determined that for this resource, there was no clearly superior value proposition. This resulted in the launch of a new, more radical test.

experiments.title: How suggested giving amounts on a donor acquisition page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which giving type would increase donor conversion rate and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2015 - 01/28/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary offered an a free online course. The goal of the free online course was for email acquisition and donor acquisition. Immediately following the course sign-up page was a donation ask.
On the donation page, a premium was offered with an open giving field. We wanted to test that page with a treatment that had suggested giving amounts next to the premium. Our hypothesis was that by adding suggested giving amounts to the donation form we would show the value of the premium and be able to increase donor conversion rate and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: DTS ran this test for a couple of months, with no signifiant results. We determined that for this donation page we would need to do a more radical test that included suggested giving amounts as one of the variables.

experiments.title: How the length of the value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more concise value proposition increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2016 - 06/19/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a new course studying Genesis. After a new user signed up, they were presented with an opportunity to make a donation supporting the course and receive a companion book.
The value proposition on the page laid out a clear reason to support the course, but the team hypothesized that two changes might increase conversion. They changed the headline from “Thank you” to “Your enrollment in Genesis is confirmed” to entice more people to read the copy. Then, they shortened the body copy to attempt to increase the force of the value proposition.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no valid lift or decrease in the conversion rate for the treatment. This tells us that DTS donors did not prefer one treatment over another, and that we must launch more radical experiments to increase the force of the value proposition.

experiments.title: How a time-sensitive value proposition affects conversion rate for nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a matching challenge ask outperform a general value proposition ask during a time sensitive campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2016 - 09/20/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary was running a fundraising campaign for North Texas Giving Day.They had previously tested into a value proposition centered around their mission: growing the kingdom. However, they wanted to test to see if a very time-sensitive specific matching challenge ask would be a greater motivator for donors and nondonors. Since the two segments had different levels of affinity and motivation, they wanted to test them separately. They created a treatment page and showed it to both donors and nondonors independently, with a 50-50 split to try to determine which value proposition was a greater motivator for this specific campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page produced a 9.5% decrease in donor conversion rate although this was not statistically valid due to low traffic for this campaign. Because it was not statistically valid, these numbers cannot be interpreted to infer that the new value proposition was less motivating to nondonors. In fact, this shows that there is practically no difference between the two. These results show that DTS must either take a more radical approach or generate more traffic in order to validate a test like this.

experiments.title: How a time-sensitive value proposition affects conversion rate for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a matching challenge ask outperform a general value proposition ask during a time sensitive campaign for a donor segment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2016 - 09/20/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary was running a fundraising campaign for North Texas Giving Day.They had previously tested into a value proposition centered around their mission: growing the kingdom. However, they wanted to test to see if a very time-sensitive specific matching challenge ask would be a greater motivator for donors and nondonors. Since the two segments had different levels of affinity and motivation, they wanted to test them separately. They created a treatment page and showed it to both donors and nondonors independently, with a 50-50 split to try to determine which value proposition was a greater motivator for this specific campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page produced a 23% decrease in donor conversion rate although this, like the nondonor test, was not statistically valid due to low traffic for this campaign. Because it was not statistically valid, these numbers cannot be interpreted to infer that the new value proposition was less motivating to donors. This test was closer to validity than the nondonor test, but even when combining the data from the two tests, there was not a clear winner or statistical validity.

experiments.title: How reduced copy and a more direct headline affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reduced copy and a more direct headline positively affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/11/2016 - 01/05/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary offered a free online course called How to Read the Bible Like a Seminary Professor to allow their donors and prospects to experience the value proposition of the Seminary. Although they had a good donor conversion rate, they wanted to see if they could improve email acquisition by making the value proposition on the signup page more appealing.
They created a treatment with a more direct headline: “Learn to read the Bible like never before”. Then, they removed the bulleted list from the copy to reduce the overall length of the copy, hypothesizing that it was not core to the value proposition on the page.
They split traffic to the page and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version produced a slight decrease in email acquisition, though it was far from valid. This shows that more radical changes need to be made to the page to significantly increase email acquisition rate.
